questions.question: NordGrid's "platform sprawl" describes the situation where:
options: Specialized tools accumulate over time — separate services for ingestion, storage, transformation, and reporting
A single team grows so large that it must split into multiple sub-teams handling overlapping responsibilities
Data volume exceeds the cluster's capacity and overflows into secondary storage tiers automatically
Cloud bills exceed budget because the provider increased prices on storage above the agreed contract
questions.correctAnswer: Specialized tools accumulate over time — separate services for ingestion, storage, transformation, and reporting
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: organizations accumulate specialized tools — Data Lake Storage for files, Databricks for transformation, Synapse for SQL — each chosen rationally but together they create duplication, security gaps, and integration costs.

questions.question: A "unified platform" means:
options: Specialized tools sharing a common foundation — especially storage, security, governance, and billing
A single tool that does ingestion, transformation, querying, and reporting all in one identical interface
A platform owned by one cloud provider so the team uses only one vendor relationship across services
A platform where every operation runs through SQL queries because SQL is the most universal interface
questions.correctAnswer: Specialized tools sharing a common foundation — especially storage, security, governance, and billing
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: unification is not "one tool does everything." Different work needs different tools (Spark for transforms, SQL for analytics). Unification means they SHARE storage, security, and governance — eliminating duplication.

questions.question: Microsoft Fabric is best described as:
options: A SaaS platform that bundles data engineering, warehousing, BI, science, and real-time into one shared foundation
A new query engine that replaces Apache Spark for distributed transformation workloads at enterprise scale
A storage-only service like Azure Data Lake Storage but with a different pricing model based on consumption
A migration tool that moves Databricks workloads to Synapse without code changes through automated translation
questions.correctAnswer: A SaaS platform that bundles data engineering, warehousing, BI, science, and real-time into one shared foundation
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Fabric is Microsoft's implementation of the unified platform — multiple workloads (Data Engineering, Data Factory, Data Warehouse, Power BI, Real-Time, Data Science) sharing OneLake, security, and capacity.

questions.question: In Fabric's SaaS model, what does Microsoft manage and what do YOU manage?
options: Microsoft manages servers, networking, scaling, and patching; YOU design data architecture, transformations, and quality
Microsoft manages everything — the data engineer's role becomes purely consultative for business stakeholders
YOU manage everything technical; Microsoft only handles billing, contracts, and Azure-level support tickets
Microsoft manages the SQL workload only; you manage Spark clusters, storage tiers, and network configuration manually
questions.correctAnswer: Microsoft manages servers, networking, scaling, and patching; YOU design data architecture, transformations, and quality
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: SaaS shifts the engineer's value from infrastructure operations (provisioning Spark clusters, configuring networking) to data architecture (which tables, what schemas, what transformations).

questions.question: Which workload would a data engineer use MOST OFTEN for transformation pipelines?
options: Data Engineering — provides Apache Spark notebooks for large-scale data processing
Real-Time Intelligence — handles streaming events through Eventstreams and KQL queries
Power BI — builds interactive reports and dashboards consumed by business users
Data Science — provides notebooks for ML model training, tracking, and registry
questions.correctAnswer: Data Engineering — provides Apache Spark notebooks for large-scale data processing
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: Data Engineering is the primary workload for Spark-based transformations. Data Factory handles orchestration. The other workloads serve different roles (analysts, ML engineers, business users).

questions.question: NordGrid's scenario splits operations into how many regions, and what are they called?
options: Four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST — each with its own meters and substations
Three voltage tiers (low / medium / high) with separate billing tables maintained per tier
Eight pricing zones derived from peak demand windows for that quarter
Two operating divisions — generation and distribution — with independent meter inventories per division
questions.correctAnswer: Four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST — each with its own meters and substations
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: NordGrid's canonical scenario uses four regions (NORTH/SOUTH/EAST/WEST). Same as the PySpark track — consistent scenario across courses helps build familiarity.

questions.question: Why does NordGrid use Bronze AND Silver, not transform raw CSV directly into Gold aggregates?
options: Reusability and debuggability — Silver serves multiple Gold tables, and raw Bronze remains as an audit replay
Spark requires three layers minimum; transforming directly to Gold violates the Catalyst optimizer's rules
Cloud providers charge less for three-layer storage than for two-layer due to compression contract terms
Three layers are mandated by GDPR for any organization processing customer-attributable data records
questions.correctAnswer: Reusability and debuggability — Silver serves multiple Gold tables, and raw Bronze remains as an audit replay
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: Silver = clean, reusable, granular. Multiple Gold tables read from one Silver. If a Gold result is questioned, you trace back through Silver to Bronze. Two layers would lose that traceability.

questions.question: In Fabric, you buy CAPACITY, not servers. What does that mean for a junior engineer's daily code?
options: Every line of code has a capacity cost — wasteful queries (reading whole tables) consume the same pool everyone shares
Capacity has unlimited scaling; engineers should optimize only when budgets are actually tight
Capacity is a manager-only concern; junior engineers should focus on logic and ignore performance entirely
Capacity is purchased per-pipeline, so each pipeline's optimization happens in isolation from others
questions.correctAnswer: Every line of code has a capacity cost — wasteful queries (reading whole tables) consume the same pool everyone shares
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: capacity = shared compute pool with a fixed size. A wasteful query slows down everyone else's work. Engineering decisions (column pruning, partition filters) directly affect platform throughput.

questions.question: A data engineer at NordGrid spends most of their time in which workspaces?
options: Bronze and Silver — where ingestion pipelines and transformation notebooks live
Gold and Real-Time — because that is where consumer-facing reports are built
Power BI workspace exclusively — engineering work has migrated to BI tools entirely
A personal workspace per engineer — collaboration happens through individual sandboxes
questions.correctAnswer: Bronze and Silver — where ingestion pipelines and transformation notebooks live
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: data engineers OWN Bronze and Silver. Gold is read access (the analyst team owns it). The engineer's domain is everything from raw arrival through validated tables.

questions.question: In the daily pipeline, what TRIGGERS the Silver transformation?
options: A Notebook activity in the Data Factory pipeline — runs after the Copy activity succeeds at delivering Bronze data
A scheduled cron job inside the notebook itself — Fabric notebooks have built-in cron syntax for self-triggering
Power BI refreshing the Gold semantic model — the refresh cascades upstream automatically through lineage
Manual click by an engineer — production transformations always require human review before each run
questions.correctAnswer: A Notebook activity in the Data Factory pipeline — runs after the Copy activity succeeds at delivering Bronze data
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: pipelines orchestrate. The Copy activity delivers raw data → Notebook activity triggers transformation → second Notebook activity computes Gold. Each step depends on the previous step's success.

questions.question: The Fabric workload switcher (top-left of the portal) is best understood as:
options: A LENS that changes what icons and tools the portal emphasizes — not a wall that restricts access
A WALL — items in one workload are inaccessible from another and switching is the only way to access them
A workspace cloner that copies items between workloads when you switch between contexts
A user-permission setting that grants different roles depending on which workload is selected
questions.correctAnswer: A LENS that changes what icons and tools the portal emphasizes — not a wall that restricts access
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: the switcher is purely cosmetic — it changes which create buttons and tutorials are highlighted. All items remain accessible regardless of which workload is selected.

questions.question: A workspace differs from a folder by:
options: Being an access-control boundary AND configuration boundary — not just a way to organize items by name
Supporting nested folders inside it — a folder is flat while a workspace is hierarchical with depth
Allowing files inside it; folders only contain pointers to items stored at the catalog's root level
Being read-only by default; folders are writable until explicitly locked through Unity Catalog policies
questions.correctAnswer: Being an access-control boundary AND configuration boundary — not just a way to organize items by name
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: a folder organizes — that's it. A workspace organizes AND controls access AND applies settings (Spark defaults, Git integration, capacity assignment). It's a much richer concept.

questions.question: When creating a workspace, what configuration MUST you set for compute-intensive items (notebooks, Lakehouses) to actually work?
options: License mode — the workspace must be assigned to a Fabric capacity, otherwise notebooks and Lakehouses won't function
A description longer than 50 characters — Unity Catalog rejects empty descriptions on creation
A primary contributor — workspaces without a designated owner are auto-archived after 7 days
Domain assignment — workspaces without a domain cannot register items in OneLake catalog
questions.correctAnswer: License mode — the workspace must be assigned to a Fabric capacity, otherwise notebooks and Lakehouses won't function
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: capacity = compute. A "Pro license only" workspace can host reports but not notebooks or Lakehouses. Wrong capacity assignment = silently broken items, no error at creation time.

questions.question: NordGrid's analysts have Viewer on Bronze, Viewer on Silver, but Contributor on Gold. Why this pattern?
options: Principle of least privilege — analysts only need to write Gold reports; broader access widens blast radius for mistakes
Spark requires Contributor on at least one workspace; Gold is the natural choice for read-write access
Fabric automatically promotes Viewer to Contributor in the workspace where most queries originate over time
Contributor on Bronze would prevent the daily pipeline from writing; Viewer is required for read access
questions.correctAnswer: Principle of least privilege — analysts only need to write Gold reports; broader access widens blast radius for mistakes
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: roles aren't about trust — they're about blast radius. An analyst with Contributor on Bronze could accidentally delete the source files. Viewer-only on data they don't own protects everyone.

questions.question: Why does Fabric AUTO-GENERATE a SQL analytics endpoint and a default semantic model when you create a Lakehouse?
options: They serve different audiences — engineers query via Spark, analysts via SQL, business users via Power BI — without separate setup
They are required for the Lakehouse to register in OneLake catalog and become discoverable to users
They serve as backup copies of the Lakehouse data in case the primary fails for any reason
They are billed separately and the auto-generation simulates a per-item billing model for finance
questions.correctAnswer: They serve different audiences — engineers query via Spark, analysts via SQL, business users via Power BI — without separate setup
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: one Lakehouse, three interfaces. Same underlying Delta tables. Engineers don't set up SQL views. Analysts don't configure Power BI sources. Business users don't learn Spark. Auto-generated bridges.

questions.question: NordGrid's convention prefixes Lakehouse items with `lh_` and notebooks with `nb_`. Why?
options: Identifies item type at a glance in lists and search results — saves cognitive overhead navigating large workspaces
Required by Unity Catalog's naming policy — items without proper prefixes are rejected at creation time
Determines which workload the item belongs to — Fabric routes operations based on the prefix character
Prevents naming collisions across workspaces — without prefixes, two items with the same name conflict globally
questions.correctAnswer: Identifies item type at a glance in lists and search results — saves cognitive overhead navigating large workspaces
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: prefixes are pure convention — Fabric doesn't enforce them. But in a workspace with 30 items, "lh_meter_readings" vs "nb_transform_meters" makes the type instantly obvious without reading the icon.

questions.question: The OneLake catalog's "Promoted" vs "Certified" endorsements differ how?
options: Promoted = team self-certifies (peer review); Certified = enterprise-level governance review by the platform team
Promoted = test environment; Certified = production environment — the labels migrate with deployment
Promoted = read-only; Certified = read-write — the labels control whether downstream pipelines can modify the item
Promoted = fast queries; Certified = slow queries — the labels determine cluster scheduling priority for that item
questions.correctAnswer: Promoted = team self-certifies (peer review); Certified = enterprise-level governance review by the platform team
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: trust ladder. Promoted = "our team checked this and it's good." Certified = "the platform team has formally reviewed and signed off on this." Different rigor, different audiences trust differently.

questions.question: Git integration is "easiest to enable when the workspace is new and empty." Why?
options: Enabling on an existing workspace requires an initial commit of all items — large workspaces produce huge first commits hard to review
Spark cannot run while Git is being configured; an empty workspace avoids any operational disruption to running pipelines
Unity Catalog rebuilds its index when Git is enabled; the rebuild is faster on empty workspaces by orders of magnitude
Git integration consumes capacity proportional to the number of items; an empty workspace minimizes the cost spike
questions.correctAnswer: Enabling on an existing workspace requires an initial commit of all items — large workspaces produce huge first commits hard to review
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: empty workspace = clean Git start, every change reviewable. Existing workspace with 50 items = a single first commit with everything, impossible to review meaningfully. Set up Git on day 1.

questions.question: A "domain" in Fabric groups workspaces by:
options: Business area (e.g. "Grid Operations", "Corporate Finance") — connects Fabric to Microsoft Purview governance
Geographic region — domains map to Azure regions for data residency compliance enforcement
Capacity SKU — domains correspond to F2/F4/F8/F64 capacity tiers for billing aggregation
Spark version — workspaces using the same Spark runtime are grouped into a runtime domain automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Business area (e.g. "Grid Operations", "Corporate Finance") — connects Fabric to Microsoft Purview governance
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: domains = business-area labels. NordGrid is in "Grid Operations". Corporate finance is "Corporate Finance". Domains link Fabric to Purview's sensitivity labels and access policies organization-wide.

questions.question: NordGrid's morning routine is to check the Monitoring Hub. The point is:
options: Verify that overnight pipelines succeeded — first step before assuming Silver/Gold tables are current
Manually trigger any pipelines that didn't run — Fabric requires daily human approval for scheduled jobs
Configure capacity for the day — capacity allocations reset every midnight and need re-application
Apply security patches to running notebooks — Spark sessions need daily restart for security compliance
questions.correctAnswer: Verify that overnight pipelines succeeded — first step before assuming Silver/Gold tables are current
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: morning routine = "did the night work?" before assuming everything is good. Catches issues before stakeholders notice. Five minutes of monitoring saves hours of incident response.

questions.question: OneLake's "OneDrive for data" analogy means:
options: One per organization, automatically provisioned, automatically governed — every Fabric workload shares it as common storage
Files are personal and private to each user; OneLake is the consumer storage layer for individual data engineers
OneLake supports drag-and-drop sync to your local desktop; that is its primary access pattern
OneLake imposes a 1 TB per-user quota similar to OneDrive's consumer plan limit policy
questions.correctAnswer: One per organization, automatically provisioned, automatically governed — every Fabric workload shares it as common storage
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: just like OneDrive (one per user, auto-provisioned), OneLake is one per ORGANIZATION (tenant). Every Fabric workload reads/writes the same lake — no separate storage accounts to provision.

questions.question: OneLake's hierarchy has 4 levels. Which is the correct order top-to-bottom?
options: Tenant → workspaces → items → files / tables (managed Delta in Tables, raw files in Files)
Tenant → catalogs → schemas → tables (mirroring traditional warehouse organization patterns)
Region → cluster → workspace → item (mirroring the underlying Azure resource hierarchy)
Domain → catalog → workspace → item (Purview-driven hierarchy for governance and lineage)
questions.correctAnswer: Tenant → workspaces → items → files / tables (managed Delta in Tables, raw files in Files)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: OneLake mirrors Fabric's organizational model. Tenant = your organization. Workspaces = teams. Items = Lakehouses, Warehouses, etc. Files/Tables sections live inside item directories.

questions.question: When you create a TABLE in a Fabric Lakehouse, what format is it stored in?
options: Delta Lake — it is the only managed table format in Fabric (and Delta files are still Parquet underneath)
Plain Parquet — Fabric does not automatically add transaction logs to managed tables in this layer
CSV by default; Delta is opt-in via a configuration setting in workspace settings
A proprietary Microsoft format that only Fabric tools can read directly without conversion
questions.correctAnswer: Delta Lake — it is the only managed table format in Fabric (and Delta files are still Parquet underneath)
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Tables section = Delta only. Delta = Parquet + a transaction log (`_delta_log`). The log gives ACID, schema enforcement, and time travel — features plain Parquet lacks.

questions.question: The Files section vs Tables section in a Lakehouse — what's the practical difference?
options: Files = unmanaged (any format, no schema); Tables = managed Delta (enforced schema, ACID transactions, SQL access)
Files = encrypted at rest; Tables = unencrypted but indexed for faster queries by analytics engines
Files = workspace-private; Tables = organization-public — files require explicit shares to expose them
Files = stored in OneLake; Tables = stored in a separate analytical store outside OneLake for performance
questions.correctAnswer: Files = unmanaged (any format, no schema); Tables = managed Delta (enforced schema, ACID transactions, SQL access)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Bronze data lands in Files (raw CSV, mixed formats). Silver/Gold tables live in Tables (Delta with schemas). The progression Files → Tables mirrors Bronze → Silver in pipeline patterns.

questions.question: A OneLake "shortcut" is best described as:
options: A symbolic link — points to data stored elsewhere; reading the shortcut reads the original location with no copy
A copy of data optimized for the destination workspace — reads happen from the local copy for performance
A snapshot of data taken at shortcut creation time — does not reflect later changes to the source data
A scheduled sync that copies data nightly — the shortcut is always slightly stale relative to its source
questions.correctAnswer: A symbolic link — points to data stored elsewhere; reading the shortcut reads the original location with no copy
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: shortcuts eliminate duplication. NordGrid's Gold workspace creates a shortcut to Silver tables instead of copying. Reads go straight to the source. One copy, many readers.

questions.question: Why does Fabric NEED both CSV and Delta as supported formats?
options: CSV is what raw sources produce (Bronze ingestion); Delta is what analytics engines need (Silver/Gold)
CSV is the proprietary Microsoft format; Delta is the open-source standard supported by other vendors only
CSV is for development; Delta is for production — the format reflects deployment maturity tier of each table
CSV is faster for analytical queries; Delta is faster for transactional updates — the tradeoff is per-pipeline
questions.correctAnswer: CSV is what raw sources produce (Bronze ingestion); Delta is what analytics engines need (Silver/Gold)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: format = pipeline stage. CSV at ingestion (Bronze), Parquet+transaction log (Delta) for processed analytical data. CSV is human-readable; Delta is fast and transactional. Match format to purpose.

questions.question: OneLake security has three LAYERS. Which layer controls access to columns containing PII (Personally Identifiable Information)?
options: Layer 2 — OneLake security supports row-level and column-level security plus narrower table-level grants
Layer 1 — workspace roles are granular enough to mask PII columns from specific users
Layer 3 — Microsoft Purview is the only mechanism that protects PII; OneLake itself has no such control
A separate fourth layer — column-level security is implemented through external tools, not in OneLake itself
questions.correctAnswer: Layer 2 — OneLake security supports row-level and column-level security plus narrower table-level grants
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: workspace roles are too broad (whole workspace). OneLake security narrows to table/row/column. Purview adds organization-wide policies (sensitivity labels). Layered defense in depth.

questions.question: Why might NordGrid use the OneLake File Explorer (Windows app) to browse Lakehouse data?
options: For ad-hoc inspection of Bronze CSV files in Windows Explorer — same interface as browsing OneDrive locally
Required for any production pipeline — Fabric pipelines must run through the file explorer to access data
Faster than the portal for analytical queries — the explorer caches data locally for 10× query speed
The file explorer is the only mechanism that bypasses Fabric capacity costs for read operations on tables
questions.correctAnswer: For ad-hoc inspection of Bronze CSV files in Windows Explorer — same interface as browsing OneDrive locally
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: external access for casual inspection. Open a CSV in Excel without going through the portal. The explorer respects security — you only see what your Fabric identity has access to.

questions.question: Compared to traditional ADLS Gen2, what does OneLake gain — and what does it LOSE?
options: Gains: deep Fabric integration (zero-config security, auto-cataloging). Loses: lower-level controls (lifecycle policies, region choice)
Gains: lower storage cost (10× cheaper than ADLS). Loses: nothing — OneLake is strictly a superset of ADLS
Gains: stronger encryption (post-quantum). Loses: cross-region replication (limited to single Azure region)
Gains: SQL-native interface for files. Loses: support for Parquet (replaced with a Microsoft proprietary format)
questions.correctAnswer: Gains: deep Fabric integration (zero-config security, auto-cataloging). Loses: lower-level controls (lifecycle policies, region choice)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: ADLS = flexible, OneLake = integrated. ADLS lets you tune everything but requires manual configuration. OneLake "just works" with Fabric but you can't customize regions or lifecycle policies as freely.

questions.question: OneLake stores files in OPEN formats (CSV, JSON, Parquet, Delta). Why is this important?
options: Your data is not locked in — any tool that reads the open format can read your OneLake data, not just Fabric
Fabric requires open formats to work; proprietary formats simply cannot be stored in OneLake at all
Open formats are faster than proprietary formats by definition; this gives Fabric a performance edge
Microsoft does not own the formats; this means Fabric receives format upgrades faster from the open community
questions.correctAnswer: Your data is not locked in — any tool that reads the open format can read your OneLake data, not just Fabric
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: deliberate design choice. Open formats mean if you ever leave Fabric, your data is portable. No vendor lock-in at the storage layer — a meaningful guarantee for enterprise adoption.

questions.question: A "Lakehouse" combines two historical concepts. What are they?
options: Data LAKE (raw files, flexible) + data WAREHOUSE (structured tables, fast queries) — Files + Tables sections
Data ENGINEER + data WAREHOUSE — combining engineering and warehousing into a unified role description
CLOUD storage + data lake — moving data lakes from on-premise to cloud-managed in unified environments
BIG-data lake + small-data house — handling both scale ranges with the same fundamental abstraction
questions.correctAnswer: Data LAKE (raw files, flexible) + data WAREHOUSE (structured tables, fast queries) — Files + Tables sections
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: lake (flexibility for raw files, any format) + warehouse (structure, fast SQL queries) = lakehouse. Files section handles the lake side, Tables section the warehouse side. One item, both modes.

questions.question: When you create a Lakehouse, you actually get THREE items. What are they?
options: The Lakehouse itself, an auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint, and a default semantic model
The Lakehouse, a backup mirror Lakehouse, and an audit log Lakehouse for compliance reporting
The Lakehouse, a Spark cluster, and a YARN queue dedicated to that Lakehouse's queries
The Lakehouse, a Power BI dashboard, and a Data Factory pipeline that orchestrates writes
questions.correctAnswer: The Lakehouse itself, an auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint, and a default semantic model
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: three siblings created together. Spark queries the Lakehouse directly. Analysts use the SQL endpoint. Power BI reports use the semantic model. All read the same Delta tables underneath.

questions.question: The Files section is "unmanaged." What does that mean concretely?
options: No schema enforcement and no Fabric catalog tracking — store any file in any format with no metadata about its structure
No backup or replication — files in this section are ephemeral and disappear after 24 hours of inactivity
No write access for the data engineer — the section is read-only and populated only by automated pipelines
No support for Delta Lake — the section can only hold CSV and JSON formats but never Parquet or Delta
questions.correctAnswer: No schema enforcement and no Fabric catalog tracking — store any file in any format with no metadata about its structure
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: unmanaged = Fabric does NOT track schemas, types, or row counts. Just a folder of files. NordGrid uses Files for raw Bronze where messy data is expected. Tables section is for the managed counterpart.

questions.question: When `df.write.saveAsTable("silver_meters")` runs in a notebook, what happens?
options: Spark writes Parquet files into the Lakehouse Tables section AND creates a Delta transaction log entry
Spark writes a CSV in the Files section; the Tables section is populated by a separate metadata service
Spark caches the DataFrame in driver memory; the actual disk write happens at session end with `spark.stop()`
Spark queues the write for the next pipeline run — saveAsTable is asynchronous and does not commit immediately
questions.correctAnswer: Spark writes Parquet files into the Lakehouse Tables section AND creates a Delta transaction log entry
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: saveAsTable produces a managed Delta table. Parquet for the data + a transaction log for the metadata. Immediately appears in the Lakehouse Tables section, the SQL endpoint, and the semantic model.

questions.question: For external sources (SFTP, Azure SQL DB), the production way to load data into a Lakehouse is:
options: A Data Factory pipeline with a Copy activity — handles authentication, scheduling, retries, and monitoring
A notebook with `requests.get()` to fetch the data — Spark provides the parallelism for any scale
Manual portal upload — the data engineer downloads to laptop, then uploads via the portal each day
A Python script run on the engineer's laptop with cron — the simplest mechanism for production scheduling
questions.correctAnswer: A Data Factory pipeline with a Copy activity — handles authentication, scheduling, retries, and monitoring
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: production = orchestrated. Pipeline Copy activity has built-in connectors, retry logic, monitoring, and integrates with the rest of the pipeline graph. Notebook downloads are dev-only.

questions.question: For a quick "did the data arrive?" check on a Silver table, the FASTEST tool is:
options: The Lakehouse explorer in the portal — opens the table preview and Schema tab in seconds
A Spark notebook — start session (30-60s), import libraries, read table, run df.show() — most thorough but slowest
Power BI — open the report and check if numbers update; covers the user-visible side of verification
Microsoft Purview lineage view — traces upstream and downstream dependencies for that table
questions.correctAnswer: The Lakehouse explorer in the portal — opens the table preview and Schema tab in seconds
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: tool match. Quick "exists?" check = explorer. Quick "right values?" check = SQL endpoint. Deep analysis = notebook. Match the tool to the question to save time.

questions.question: The SQL Analytics Endpoint is "read-only." What does that mean for analysts?
options: They can SELECT, GROUP BY, JOIN, create views — but cannot INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or CREATE TABLE
They can only run queries that return less than 10 MB of data; larger result sets require the Warehouse
They can access only the first 100 rows of any table; full reads require switching to a Spark notebook session
They cannot use any T-SQL features; queries are restricted to a Microsoft-defined "safe SQL" subset
questions.correctAnswer: They can SELECT, GROUP BY, JOIN, create views — but cannot INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or CREATE TABLE
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: read-only = no data modification. Only the engineer's notebooks/pipelines can write tables (consistency guarantee). Analysts get full SELECT power including views, but writes are off-limits.

questions.question: Why does the default semantic model use "Direct Lake" mode instead of Import or DirectQuery?
options: Direct Lake reads OneLake Delta files directly — no data import (always fresh) and no per-query database hits (fast)
Direct Lake compresses the data 10× more aggressively than Import; it is the most storage-efficient option always
Direct Lake is required for any semantic model larger than 1 GB; smaller models can use Import for speed
Direct Lake is the only mode supported by Power BI free tier; the other modes require Premium licensing tiers
questions.correctAnswer: Direct Lake reads OneLake Delta files directly — no data import (always fresh) and no per-query database hits (fast)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: Import = fast queries, stale data. DirectQuery = fresh data, slow queries. Direct Lake = both — reads Delta files in OneLake on demand, fast and fresh. Unique to Fabric.

questions.question: NordGrid's rule "one Lakehouse per layer per domain" means:
options: Bronze + Silver + Gold = three Lakehouses per data domain (meter readings, SCADA, weather, etc.)
One Lakehouse per workspace — each workspace can hold exactly one Lakehouse to simplify lineage tracking
Layer assignment is automatic based on the data's sensitivity classification from Microsoft Purview policies
Domains and Lakehouses are 1:1 — there is exactly one Lakehouse per business domain in the entire tenant
questions.correctAnswer: Bronze + Silver + Gold = three Lakehouses per data domain (meter readings, SCADA, weather, etc.)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: granularity rule. Domain (data type) × layer (medallion stage) = one Lakehouse. Meter readings has Bronze/Silver/Gold; SCADA has its own Bronze/Silver/Gold. Clear boundaries, clear ownership.

questions.question: A Lakehouse is described as "the foundation that everything stands on" because:
options: Notebooks read/write it, pipelines copy into it, SQL endpoint queries it, Power BI reports it — central to the data flow
Fabric requires every workspace to have at least one Lakehouse; no Lakehouse means no items can be created
It is the only item type that supports schema enforcement; everything else inherits its schema definitions automatically
It is billed separately from other items, making it the cost-allocation root for the entire workspace
questions.correctAnswer: Notebooks read/write it, pipelines copy into it, SQL endpoint queries it, Power BI reports it — central to the data flow
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Lakehouse = the hub. Every data engineering tool ties back to it. Master the Lakehouse and the rest of Fabric becomes a coherent toolkit on top of it.

questions.question: A Fabric notebook is best described as:
options: A document that interleaves code cells with markdown — code runs interactively with results shown immediately below
A SQL editor — only T-SQL queries are supported, with results saved as views automatically
A pipeline configuration file — defines what activities run and in what order through visual cards
A code repository — stores Python files and gets compiled into a deployment artifact for production
questions.correctAnswer: A document that interleaves code cells with markdown — code runs interactively with results shown immediately below
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: notebook = code cells + markdown + interactive execution. Familiar to anyone who used Jupyter. Fabric's notebooks add managed Spark, Lakehouse integration, and pipeline trigger support.

questions.question: When creating a Fabric notebook, what's the FIRST configuration step beyond naming?
options: Attaching a Lakehouse to the notebook — without this, code that references Lakehouse tables fails
Creating a Spark cluster — Fabric notebooks require manual cluster provisioning before running any code
Configuring authentication — every notebook needs explicit OAuth setup for OneLake access
Choosing a Python version — Fabric supports Python 3.8 through 3.11 with explicit selection per notebook
questions.correctAnswer: Attaching a Lakehouse to the notebook — without this, code that references Lakehouse tables fails
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Lakehouse attachment = which storage your notebook reads/writes by default. spark.read.table("silver_meters") looks up the table in the attached Lakehouse. Without it, you get "table not found".

questions.question: In a Fabric notebook, the `spark` variable is:
options: A pre-configured SparkSession ready to use — no need to call SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate() at the start
A placeholder until you call spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("...").getOrCreate() in the first cell
A read-only configuration object — you can't use it directly; you must wrap it in your own session
A connection to a remote Spark cluster — you must authenticate before any operations actually work
questions.correctAnswer: A pre-configured SparkSession ready to use — no need to call SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate() at the start
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Fabric pre-builds the session. NordGrid's code starts with `df = spark.read.table(...)` — no session creation needed. Pasting standalone PySpark code with `SparkSession.builder` is a common mistake.

questions.question: A Fabric notebook supports MIXING which languages?
options: PySpark (default), Spark SQL (`%%sql`), Scala (`%%scala`), and R (`%%sparkr`) — different cells can use different languages
Only PySpark — Fabric notebooks are Python-exclusive for transformation work
PySpark and SQL only — Scala and R are reserved for the Data Science workload, not Data Engineering
PySpark + JavaScript — JS is the only secondary language for client-side transformations in notebooks
questions.correctAnswer: PySpark (default), Spark SQL (`%%sql`), Scala (`%%scala`), and R (`%%sparkr`) — different cells can use different languages
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: cell-level magic commands switch languages. Useful pattern: PySpark for transformation cells, %%sql for verification queries at the end. Pick one primary language per notebook for readability.

questions.question: NordGrid sets `inferSchema=False` and provides an explicit `StructType` schema in production. Why?
options: Inference is slow on large files AND non-deterministic — explicit schemas prevent silent type changes from source drift
Inference is forbidden in Fabric notebooks; setting it raises a runtime error before the read finishes
Inference triples the storage cost; explicit schemas are needed for cost control in any production pipeline
Inference produces identical results to explicit schemas; the convention is for code-review consistency only
questions.correctAnswer: Inference is slow on large files AND non-deterministic — explicit schemas prevent silent type changes from source drift
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: inference scans the file twice and guesses based on sample. If a column starts containing decimals later, the type silently flips. Explicit schema = deterministic contract.

questions.question: NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses `mode("overwrite")` to write to the Silver Lakehouse. Why overwrite?
options: The daily transformation reads ALL Bronze files for the processing window and rewrites the Silver table completely
Overwrite is the only mode supported by Delta Lake in Fabric for managed tables
Overwrite is faster than append because it skips the transaction log update step entirely
Append would require reconfiguring the SQL endpoint after every write, which costs additional capacity
questions.correctAnswer: The daily transformation reads ALL Bronze files for the processing window and rewrites the Silver table completely
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: overwrite makes the daily run idempotent. If yesterday's run had a bug, today's run replaces both yesterday and today's output. Append would accumulate with no clean redo path.

questions.question: For PRODUCTION notebooks, libraries should be installed via:
options: A Fabric Environment item — published once, attached to notebooks, version-pinned, repeatable across runs
`%pip install` at the top of each notebook — quick and self-contained per script with no external dependencies
Loading from `/usr/local/lib` on each executor — avoids any external dependency at runtime entirely
Compiling into the notebook itself as bytecode — the most performant option for repeated executions
questions.correctAnswer: A Fabric Environment item — published once, attached to notebooks, version-pinned, repeatable across runs
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: %pip = dev shortcut. Production needs reproducibility. Environments pin versions, get reviewed, and live in Git. Without Environments, "works today, breaks tomorrow when PyPI changes" is a real failure mode.

questions.question: Notebook cells share state within the Spark session. What's the implication?
options: Variables in cell 1 are available in cell 5 — but if you run out of order, state may not match the code's reading
Cells are isolated; passing data between cells requires writing to a temporary file in the Files section
Each cell gets a fresh SparkSession — performance is consistent because state never accumulates over time
Variables are auto-cleared after 60 seconds of inactivity to keep memory usage bounded for long sessions
questions.correctAnswer: Variables in cell 1 are available in cell 5 — but if you run out of order, state may not match the code's reading
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: shared state = cell ordering matters. During development, you may run cell 5 before cell 3 to debug. Before commit, ALWAYS Run All to verify the notebook works top-to-bottom.

questions.question: A "parameter cell" at the top of the notebook does what?
options: Defines variables (e.g. `processing_date`) that the pipeline can OVERRIDE at run time — bridges interactive and automated runs
Loads parameters from a config file at every run — eliminates hardcoded values from the notebook itself
Sets up the Spark session's memory and core counts — a notebook-level capacity override mechanism
Configures Power BI export options for the notebook output — required for any notebook visualizations to render
questions.correctAnswer: Defines variables (e.g. `processing_date`) that the pipeline can OVERRIDE at run time — bridges interactive and automated runs
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: parameters = automation bridge. Default values during interactive dev (today's date). Pipeline overrides at runtime (yesterday's date for backfill). Same notebook, different inputs.

questions.question: A notebook becomes "production-ready" when:
options: It has explicit schemas, validation cells, parameters, no exploratory show() calls, and is committed to Git
It runs without errors at least once during interactive development by the original author
It contains at least 100 lines of code; shorter notebooks are considered prototypes only
It has been refactored into Python script form; notebooks are never deployed directly to production
questions.correctAnswer: It has explicit schemas, validation cells, parameters, no exploratory show() calls, and is committed to Git
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: production-ready = repeatable, reviewable, automated. Schema enforces structure. Validation catches bad data. Parameters enable backfill. Git enables review. Removed show() reduces wasted compute.

questions.question: The transformation pipeline runs in a SEQUENCE of steps. Why does the ORDER matter?
options: Earlier steps (schema enforcement, filtering) reduce data; later steps (deduplication, derivations) work on cleaner inputs
Spark requires a fixed order; deviations cause Catalyst to fall back to interpreted execution — much slower
The order determines partition layout — wrong order produces unreadable Parquet files in OneLake
Order affects only readability, not correctness — Catalyst auto-reorders steps for optimal execution every time
questions.correctAnswer: Earlier steps (schema enforcement, filtering) reduce data; later steps (deduplication, derivations) work on cleaner inputs
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: order = data quality compounding. Filter out garbage first → less data to deduplicate → faster derivations. Doing derivations first then filtering wastes compute on rows you'll discard.

questions.question: NordGrid defines a `StructType` schema before reading the CSV. Schema enforcement catches:
options: Missing columns, extra columns, wrong types, and unexpected nullability — STRUCTURAL problems, not value problems
All data quality issues including out-of-range values, formatting errors, and impossible combinations
Only column count mismatches — type mismatches are silently coerced to the schema-specified type
Only top-level columns — nested struct fields are not validated by Spark's schema enforcement layer
questions.correctAnswer: Missing columns, extra columns, wrong types, and unexpected nullability — STRUCTURAL problems, not value problems
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: structure ≠ quality. Schema catches "the column structure is wrong." Quality (value 999999 for daily_kw is impossible) requires separate filter() / quality gate logic.

questions.question: NordGrid's 5 quality filters are CHAINED in one expression. Performance impact vs separate `filter()` calls?
options: No difference — Catalyst combines chained filters into a single scan regardless of how they're written in code
Chained is 5× faster — Spark internally treats one filter call differently from multiple calls
Separate is faster — each filter() call provides explicit checkpoint boundaries for parallelization
Chained is slower — Spark cannot optimize across multiple conditions in a single filter call
questions.correctAnswer: No difference — Catalyst combines chained filters into a single scan regardless of how they're written in code
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: optimizer-level decision. Whether you write `df.filter(A & B & C)` or `df.filter(A).filter(B).filter(C)`, Catalyst combines them. Choose for readability, not performance.

questions.question: NordGrid uses `withColumn("year", year(col("date")))` to derive a `year` column. The MAIN benefit is:
options: Enables partition pruning — `partitionBy("year", "month")` later organizes Parquet files for fast time-range filtering
Reduces storage size — derived columns compress better than raw date strings in Delta tables typically
Required by the SQL endpoint — date columns without year derivation are not queryable through T-SQL
Improves Power BI rendering speed — Power BI displays year columns 10× faster than full timestamps
questions.correctAnswer: Enables partition pruning — `partitionBy("year", "month")` later organizes Parquet files for fast time-range filtering
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: year/month derivation has a downstream payoff. Partitioning by year/month lets queries filtering by date skip directories, dramatically reducing scan time.

questions.question: When `daily_kw` is null, what does `daily_kw / 1000` evaluate to?
options: null — null propagates through arithmetic; the result is also null with no exception or default value
0 — Spark replaces null with zero before arithmetic operations to maintain numeric expression validity
NaN — Spark converts null to NaN for floating-point operations to comply with IEEE 754 standard semantics
An exception — Spark raises NullArithmeticException to surface data quality issues before they propagate
questions.correctAnswer: null — null propagates through arithmetic; the result is also null with no exception or default value
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: nulls propagate silently. `null / 1000` = null. `null + 5` = null. `coalesce(daily_kw, 0)` substitutes a default before math. Without coalesce, downstream aggregations may produce unexpected nulls.

questions.question: Why does NordGrid TRIM string columns (`trim(upper(col("region")))`) before deduplication?
options: Otherwise "NORTH" and "NORTH " (trailing space) are treated as DIFFERENT region values by Spark's comparison
Untrimmed strings consume 50% more storage in Parquet — trimming is purely a compression optimization
Spark cannot apply column statistics to untrimmed strings, slowing down subsequent partition pruning operations
The SQL analytics endpoint refuses to register columns with leading or trailing whitespace in any row
questions.correctAnswer: Otherwise "NORTH" and "NORTH " (trailing space) are treated as DIFFERENT region values by Spark's comparison
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: standardize before dedupe. Untrimmed strings produce silent bugs — joins miss matches, groupBy splits a region into two groups, dedupe keeps both variants. Trim/upper first.

questions.question: `dropDuplicates(["meter_id", "date"])` differs from `distinct()` how?
options: dropDuplicates uses a SUBSET of columns as the key; distinct() requires all columns to match for two rows to be duplicates
dropDuplicates is faster but less accurate; distinct() is slower but produces deterministic results across runs
dropDuplicates only works on Delta tables; distinct() works on any DataFrame including raw CSV reads
They are functionally identical; dropDuplicates is just an alias kept for backward compatibility
questions.correctAnswer: dropDuplicates uses a SUBSET of columns as the key; distinct() requires all columns to match for two rows to be duplicates
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: composite key matters. NordGrid's rule: "one reading per meter per date" → dedupe by (meter_id, date). distinct() would only dedupe rows where EVERY column matched — too strict.

questions.question: NordGrid writes Silver with `partitionBy("year", "month")`. The downstream benefit is:
options: Queries filtering by year/month skip non-matching partition directories at the file system level
Parquet files become 50% smaller because partitioning enables better dictionary encoding overall
The Spark UI displays partition statistics inline — required for performance debugging in production
Power BI auto-generates date hierarchies based on partition columns rather than column metadata
questions.correctAnswer: Queries filtering by year/month skip non-matching partition directories at the file system level
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: partition pruning. `WHERE year=2025 AND month=03` reads only that one directory. Without partitioning, Spark scans all files and filters in memory — much slower.

questions.question: NordGrid's validation cells run AFTER the Silver write. The role of validation is:
options: Catch business-logic problems unit tests miss — wrong row counts, unexpected nulls, missing regions, anomalous values
Replace unit tests entirely — validation queries are easier to maintain than separate test files
Verify infrastructure — confirm Spark started, Lakehouse mounted, and the SQL endpoint registered
Authenticate the engineer — the validation cell prompts for credentials before allowing the next pipeline step
questions.correctAnswer: Catch business-logic problems unit tests miss — wrong row counts, unexpected nulls, missing regions, anomalous values
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: unit tests verify functions. Validation verifies the OUTPUT is plausible. "Did we get ~500K rows?" "Are all 4 regions present?" Catches issues that pass unit tests but fail in production data.

questions.question: NordGrid's entire Silver transformation notebook is approximately how many cells?
options: Around 6 cells — parameters, imports + schema, read, transform, write, validate
Around 50 cells — one per derivation, one per filter, one per type cast for fine-grained debugging
Around 100 cells — a separate cell for every line of code to enable line-by-line execution
Just 1 cell — production code is best written as a single block to ensure all-or-nothing execution
questions.correctAnswer: Around 6 cells — parameters, imports + schema, read, transform, write, validate
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: clean structure. Each cell has a clear job. Easy to review. Easy to debug. Six logical phases is more maintainable than fifty micro-cells or one mega-cell.

questions.question: Notebooks contain TRANSFORMATION logic. Pipelines contain WHAT?
options: ORCHESTRATION logic — sequence of activities, dependencies, schedules, parameter passing, monitoring
TRANSFORMATION logic in a visual format — pipelines are the no-code equivalent of notebook code
STORAGE configuration — pipelines define where data lives (which Lakehouse, which workspace) automatically
POWER BI configuration — pipelines define how reports refresh after data updates upstream in the workflow
questions.correctAnswer: ORCHESTRATION logic — sequence of activities, dependencies, schedules, parameter passing, monitoring
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: notebook = "what to do with one batch of data." Pipeline = "when to run the notebook, with what parameters, after which other steps, and what to do if it fails." Different concerns, different tools.

questions.question: Fabric pipelines use a VISUAL canvas. Compared to Airflow's Python-code DAGs, this means:
options: Lower-code: drag-and-drop activities, configure properties — no Python required for orchestration
Higher performance: visual pipelines compile to optimized C++ at runtime for faster execution
Restricted features: only basic activities are supported; advanced patterns require code escape hatches
Required: Fabric does not support code-based pipeline definitions, even for advanced users wanting flexibility
questions.correctAnswer: Lower-code: drag-and-drop activities, configure properties — no Python required for orchestration
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: design choice. Visual canvas = lower learning curve, slower for complex DAGs. Python code = steeper curve, faster for power users. Each suits different teams and complexity levels.

questions.question: The COPY activity is for what specifically?
options: Moving data from external sources (SFTP, SQL DB, REST API) into a Fabric Lakehouse — without transformation
Copying data between two notebooks — passing DataFrames as parameters in the activity's payload
Replicating Power BI reports across workspaces — for staging-to-production deployment patterns
Cloning the entire Lakehouse — used for backup before any schema migration or risky update operation
questions.correctAnswer: Moving data from external sources (SFTP, SQL DB, REST API) into a Fabric Lakehouse — without transformation
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Copy = ingestion. Authentication, retries, scheduling, dynamic file paths — but NO transformation. The CSV that goes in is the CSV that comes out, just relocated to OneLake.

questions.question: Why does the Notebook activity in a pipeline OVERRIDE the notebook's default parameters?
options: Lets the pipeline pass runtime context (today's date, current workspace) — same notebook adapts to different runs
Required for security — pipelines must explicitly authorize each parameter rather than inheriting defaults
For performance — the override skips reading the notebook's parameter cell on every run, saving milliseconds
Optional — most pipelines run notebooks with default parameters; override is for edge-case backfills only
questions.correctAnswer: Lets the pipeline pass runtime context (today's date, current workspace) — same notebook adapts to different runs
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: parameters connect dev to production. Notebook defaults during interactive dev (today). Pipeline overrides for daily runs (yesterday) and backfills (any date). One notebook, many run contexts.

questions.question: When should you use the DATAFLOW activity instead of the NOTEBOOK activity?
options: Simple ref-data refreshes (200 rows, low-code transforms) — dataflows have lower overhead than starting Spark
Heavy-lifting transformations on millions of rows — dataflows scale better than Spark for large analytical workloads
When the pipeline runs on a small Fabric capacity — dataflows are required to fit within F2 capacity limits
Always — Microsoft has officially deprecated notebook-based transformations in favor of dataflows since the 2024 release
questions.correctAnswer: Simple ref-data refreshes (200 rows, low-code transforms) — dataflows have lower overhead than starting Spark
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: each tool fits a niche. Dataflow Gen2 = Power Query for small reference data. Notebook = PySpark for heavy transforms. NordGrid uses both, picking the right tool per job.

questions.question: Activity dependency arrows connect activities. The four CONDITIONS are:
options: On Success, On Failure, On Completion, On Skipped — different colors in the canvas (green, red, blue, gray)
Sequential, Parallel, Conditional, Looping — control-flow primitives borrowed from programming languages
Always, Daily, Weekly, Monthly — temporal triggers for the next activity to run on its own schedule
Read, Write, Update, Delete — data-mutation conditions that determine which activity runs next
questions.correctAnswer: On Success, On Failure, On Completion, On Skipped — different colors in the canvas (green, red, blue, gray)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: Success = run if previous succeeded. Failure = run if previous failed (e.g. send Slack alert). Completion = run regardless. Skipped = run if previous didn't even start. Four colors = four conditions.

questions.question: NordGrid's SFTP file lands at 01:30 UTC. Why does NordGrid schedule the pipeline for 02:00 UTC, not 01:30?
options: A safety MARGIN — sources are sometimes late, networks sometimes slow; 30 minutes prevents flaky failures
Spark needs 30 minutes to warm up before any pipeline can start execution efficiently
Capacity reservations align to 30-minute boundaries — 01:30 isn't a valid scheduling slot in Fabric
The SQL analytics endpoint refreshes at 02:00 UTC; pipelines must run AFTER the refresh completes
questions.correctAnswer: A safety MARGIN — sources are sometimes late, networks sometimes slow; 30 minutes prevents flaky failures
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: schedule with cushion. Production sources occasionally arrive a few minutes late. Scheduling exactly when the file is expected = transient failures. Schedule with margin = reliability.

questions.question: Pipeline expressions like `@formatDateTime(utcNow(), "yyyy-MM-dd")` are evaluated WHEN?
options: At RUNTIME — typos and missing functions surface only when the pipeline runs (not at design time)
At design time — the visual canvas validates expressions immediately and rejects errors before save
On parameter creation only — once defined, the value is frozen for the pipeline's lifetime regardless
Once per workspace — each workspace caches expression results across all its pipelines for a day
questions.correctAnswer: At RUNTIME — typos and missing functions surface only when the pipeline runs (not at design time)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: runtime evaluation = silent design-time errors. A typo in `formatDateTimee` shows up only when the pipeline executes. Always test with a manual run before scheduling.

questions.question: When investigating a failed pipeline, the FIRST step is:
options: Identify WHICH activity failed via the activity-level detail in the monitoring hub — not assume it's the notebook
Restart the workspace — most failures are transient and a restart resolves them automatically
Email the platform team — pipeline failures require human escalation as standard procedure for any error
Examine the cluster logs first — pipelines fail because of cluster issues 90% of the time empirically
questions.correctAnswer: Identify WHICH activity failed via the activity-level detail in the monitoring hub — not assume it's the notebook
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: triage by activity. Copy failed = source issue. Notebook failed = transformation bug. Dataflow failed = ref data issue. Each has different root causes and fixes — identify which first.

questions.question: NordGrid's daily pipeline starts at 02:00 UTC. By 02:17, what's the expected state?
options: Bronze ingestion done, Silver transformation done, Gold aggregation done — dashboard ready hours before the team arrives
Bronze partial — large file ingestion takes until at least 04:00 UTC for the daily volume
Pipeline still queued — Fabric pipelines wait for capacity headroom before running production jobs
All steps complete and Power BI report rendered — but emails to the team go at 08:00 UTC always
questions.correctAnswer: Bronze ingestion done, Silver transformation done, Gold aggregation done — dashboard ready hours before the team arrives
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: short pipeline. Copy ~4 min, Silver notebook ~12 min, Gold notebook ~3 min = ~17 min total. Dashboard is fresh at 02:17, six hours before the operations team arrives at 08:00.

questions.question: Dataflow Gen2 is built on POWER QUERY. The familiarity advantage is:
options: Excel and Power BI users already know Power Query — same skills transfer directly to Fabric dataflows
Power Query is the fastest transformation engine available; speed is the primary reason for adoption
Power Query is the only Microsoft technology with the Apache 2.0 license; this is required for cloud deployment
Power Query auto-generates pipeline code — dataflows are just visual frontends to existing Python scripts
questions.correctAnswer: Excel and Power BI users already know Power Query — same skills transfer directly to Fabric dataflows
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: skill reuse. Analysts who learned Power Query in Excel/Power BI immediately use Fabric dataflows. Lower onboarding friction for low-code users.

questions.question: Power Query's "applied steps" pane is essentially:
options: A REVERSIBLE LOG of every transformation — click any step to see data at that point, delete steps to undo
A read-only history — for audit purposes only; transformations cannot be undone once applied to the query
A list of available transformations the user can apply — clicking adds the step to the query
A debugging-only feature; it is hidden in production dataflows for performance reasons
questions.correctAnswer: A REVERSIBLE LOG of every transformation — click any step to see data at that point, delete steps to undo
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: applied steps = transformation history. Each transformation generates a step. Step deletion is undo. Reordering steps changes execution. Self-documenting and explorable.

questions.question: When connecting a dataflow to an external SOURCE, you configure:
options: A connection (saved credential) — same connection can be reused by other dataflows and pipelines in the workspace
A direct username and password in the dataflow itself — credentials are encoded in the dataflow definition
A Spark JDBC URL — Power Query uses Spark-style connectors under the hood for all sources
A SQL view in the source database — dataflows can only read from SQL views, never tables directly
questions.correctAnswer: A connection (saved credential) — same connection can be reused by other dataflows and pipelines in the workspace
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: connection = saved credential. Configure once, reuse many times. The credential is hidden — pipeline authors reference the connection by name without seeing the password.

questions.question: In Power Query, "Replace Values" maps `N` → `NORTH`. The PySpark equivalent is:
options: `when(col("region") == "N", "NORTH").otherwise(col("region"))` — the conditional CASE WHEN pattern
`col("region").replace("N", "NORTH")` — the direct string replacement on the Column object
`upper(col("region"))` — string upper-casing happens to produce the same standardization output
`fillna({"region": "NORTH"})` — null replacement is the closest direct PySpark analog to Replace Values
questions.correctAnswer: `when(col("region") == "N", "NORTH").otherwise(col("region"))` — the conditional CASE WHEN pattern
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Power Query Replace Values = SQL CASE WHEN = PySpark when().otherwise(). Same operation, three syntaxes. Fluency in one helps you map to the others.

questions.question: "Merge Queries" in Power Query is the visual equivalent of:
options: A SQL JOIN / PySpark `df.join(other, key)` — combines rows from two queries based on matching key values
A SQL UNION / PySpark `df.union(other)` — stacks rows from two queries vertically into one combined result
A SQL GROUP BY / PySpark `df.groupBy(key).agg(...)` — collapses rows by a key column for aggregation
A SQL WHERE / PySpark `df.filter(...)` — filters one query based on values present in another query
questions.correctAnswer: A SQL JOIN / PySpark `df.join(other, key)` — combines rows from two queries based on matching key values
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: Merge = join (combine columns from two queries by key). Append = union (stack rows from two queries). Different operations, same visual interface in Power Query Editor.

questions.question: A Dataflow Gen2 with a Lakehouse destination set to "Replace" mode does what on each refresh?
options: DROPS the existing table and recreates it with fresh data — same as `df.write.mode("overwrite")` in PySpark
APPENDS new rows to the existing table — same as `df.write.mode("append")` in PySpark
UPDATES rows where keys match, INSERTS new ones — same as Delta MERGE upsert pattern
BACKS UP the existing table before writing new data — keeps old version for time-travel queries
questions.correctAnswer: DROPS the existing table and recreates it with fresh data — same as `df.write.mode("overwrite")` in PySpark
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: Replace = overwrite. Used for reference data that's fully refreshed on each run. Append is for fact-data that accumulates. Match the mode to the data's update pattern.

questions.question: Visual Power Query transformations are converted to what underneath?
options: M language code — a functional programming language designed specifically for data transformation queries
PySpark code — Power Query is a UI on top of Spark for low-code engineers preferring visual workflows
SQL code — Power Query generates T-SQL queries that the SQL endpoint executes against the source
Java bytecode — Power Query compiles directly to JVM bytecode for performance reasons
questions.correctAnswer: M language code — a functional programming language designed specifically for data transformation queries
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: visual = M code. Click-driven steps generate equivalent M code (visible via Advanced Editor). You don't need to write M, but reading it helps debug unexpected behavior.

questions.question: The KEY decision factor for "Dataflow vs Notebook" is:
options: Complexity — Dataflow for sequential filter-rename-typecast-join chains; Notebook for code, conditionals, complex logic
Data size — Dataflows for >1 GB; notebooks for <1 GB. Dataflows have higher parallelism than Spark for big data
Author role — Dataflows are for data engineers; notebooks are for data scientists. Roles map 1:1 to tools
Cluster type — Dataflows run on dedicated SQL clusters; notebooks need Spark clusters. Different infrastructure
questions.correctAnswer: Complexity — Dataflow for sequential filter-rename-typecast-join chains; Notebook for code, conditionals, complex logic
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: simplicity test. If the transform fits in a sequential step list, dataflow is simpler. If it needs loops, conditionals, custom functions, complex SQL — notebook is the right tool.

questions.question: NordGrid uses dataflows for ~20% of transformation steps. What kind of work falls into that 20%?
options: Reference-data refreshes — small tables (substations: 200 rows, weather: 50 rows) from SQL/SharePoint sources
All Bronze ingestion — dataflows are the entry point for raw data; notebooks only handle Silver and Gold
Power BI report generation — dataflows render the visual reports that the analytics team consumes
Streaming workloads — Real-Time Intelligence requires dataflows; notebooks handle batch only
questions.correctAnswer: Reference-data refreshes — small tables (substations: 200 rows, weather: 50 rows) from SQL/SharePoint sources
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: NordGrid's split. 80% notebooks (heavy Silver/Gold transformations on millions of rows). 20% dataflows (reference data and ad-hoc imports). The split reflects each tool's strengths.

questions.question: A scheduled dataflow refresh "continues even if you forget about it." The implication:
options: TURN OFF refresh when the dataflow is no longer needed — orphaned refreshes consume capacity indefinitely
Refreshes auto-disable after 30 days of no human interaction with the dataflow
Disabled refreshes don't affect billing — capacity is only billed for executing transformations, not idle schedules
Refreshes can't be turned off without admin approval — by design to prevent stale data in production
questions.correctAnswer: TURN OFF refresh when the dataflow is no longer needed — orphaned refreshes consume capacity indefinitely
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: scheduled refreshes are sticky. NordGrid's test dataflow refreshing hourly during dev kept running for months — wasted capacity. Disable at end-of-test or set explicit end dates on schedules.

questions.question: Fabric provides TWO SQL interfaces: SQL endpoint and Warehouse. The KEY difference?
options: Endpoint = read-only T-SQL on Lakehouse Delta tables. Warehouse = full read-write T-SQL with CREATE/INSERT/UPDATE
Endpoint = T-SQL only. Warehouse = T-SQL + Spark + Python + R — multi-language analytical engine
Endpoint = free. Warehouse = paid premium feature requiring an additional license per query
Endpoint = batch-only. Warehouse = supports streaming queries with sub-second latency for live data
questions.correctAnswer: Endpoint = read-only T-SQL on Lakehouse Delta tables. Warehouse = full read-write T-SQL with CREATE/INSERT/UPDATE
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: endpoint reads what notebooks wrote. Warehouse is its own writable SQL store. Endpoint = "give analysts SQL access to my Delta tables." Warehouse = "let SQL teams own their own tables."

questions.question: A new Delta table from a notebook (`saveAsTable("silver_meters")`) appears in the SQL endpoint:
options: AUTOMATICALLY within seconds — the endpoint detects all Tables-section Delta tables on registration
Only after manual catalog refresh — analysts must click "Refresh" in the SQL editor to see new tables
Only on the next workspace restart — table registration happens at startup, not at runtime
Never — Delta tables created by notebooks must be explicitly registered to the SQL endpoint via DDL
questions.correctAnswer: AUTOMATICALLY within seconds — the endpoint detects all Tables-section Delta tables on registration
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: zero-config for analysts. Engineer writes a Delta table → analyst queries it within seconds. No DDL, no refresh button, no permissions setup beyond workspace roles.

questions.question: The SQL endpoint is READ-ONLY. If you try `INSERT INTO silver_meters ...`, what happens?
options: The query FAILS with a permission error — INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE are blocked by design
The query succeeds and writes to a temporary table, then prompts you to confirm a real write
The query succeeds — the read-only restriction was lifted in Fabric's November 2024 update for owners
The query queues — INSERTs through the endpoint are batched and committed at the next pipeline run
questions.correctAnswer: The query FAILS with a permission error — INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE are blocked by design
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: design enforcement. Only notebooks/pipelines write Lakehouse tables → consistent ownership. Allowing analyst INSERTs would create accountability ambiguity ("who put this row here?").

questions.question: Why does NordGrid create VIEWS in the SQL endpoint instead of letting each analyst write the same query?
options: Centralizes the query logic — change the view definition once and every consumer benefits without updating their reports
Required by Power BI — semantic models can only reference views, not tables directly
Faster execution — views materialize results to disk and cache them across analyst sessions
Required by Microsoft Purview — sensitivity labels apply to views only, not underlying Lakehouse tables
questions.correctAnswer: Centralizes the query logic — change the view definition once and every consumer benefits without updating their reports
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: views as data contracts. "v_monthly_region_summary" is the analyst's interface. Engineer changes underlying logic; the view name stays — analysts unaffected.

questions.question: NordGrid's Gold layer uses BOTH a Lakehouse and a Warehouse. Why both?
options: PySpark notebooks write the Lakehouse (simple aggregates); T-SQL writes the Warehouse (joins, stored procedures, billing)
For redundancy — if one fails, the other serves as a backup with the same data automatically
Required by Fabric — Gold workspaces must contain both items for licensing compliance reasons
For different consumers — Lakehouse for analysts, Warehouse for executives. Each role gets a dedicated tool
questions.correctAnswer: PySpark notebooks write the Lakehouse (simple aggregates); T-SQL writes the Warehouse (joins, stored procedures, billing)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: pick the language that fits the work. NordGrid uses both because some Gold tables are best computed in PySpark, others in T-SQL. Same Gold layer, two engines side-by-side.

questions.question: NordGrid's billing table is in the WAREHOUSE (not Lakehouse). The pattern is `INSERT INTO ... SELECT FROM ...`. Why?
options: Billing logic naturally expresses as SQL (joins, GROUP BY, CASE WHEN); Warehouse handles the SQL-native workflow well
Warehouses are required for any financial data per SOX compliance — Lakehouses are not certified for billing
Warehouses execute SQL 10× faster than the Lakehouse SQL endpoint for joins between two tables
Lakehouses do not support INSERT — only saveAsTable from notebooks. Warehouse is the only way to write SQL
questions.correctAnswer: Billing logic naturally expresses as SQL (joins, GROUP BY, CASE WHEN); Warehouse handles the SQL-native workflow well
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: the right tool for the team. SQL-fluent teams write billing as a CTAS query. PySpark-fluent teams write the same logic in a notebook. Both produce the same Gold table — different paths.

questions.question: NordGrid's Warehouse joins `silver_meters` (Silver Lakehouse) with `dim_regions` (Gold Warehouse) in ONE query. The KEY enabler:
options: Cross-database queries — Fabric supports joins across Lakehouses and Warehouses via three-part naming
A separate ETL step that copies Silver into the Warehouse before the join — required for cross-database access
A foreign-key relationship defined in Unity Catalog — only declared FK relationships allow cross-database joins
A custom Power BI semantic model that pre-materializes the join result — required for any cross-database reads
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-database queries — Fabric supports joins across Lakehouses and Warehouses via three-part naming
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: cross-database = no copying. `lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters` JOIN `dw_nordgrid_analytics.dbo.dim_regions` in one statement. The data stays in OneLake; the SQL engine handles the federation.

questions.question: T-SQL's `YEAR(date)` corresponds to PySpark's:
options: `year(col("date"))` — function names match almost 1:1 between SQL and PySpark for date operations
`extract("year", col("date"))` — PySpark uses extract() universally for all date-part operations
`col("date").year()` — date columns expose date-part methods for direct extraction in PySpark
`to_year(col("date"))` — PySpark uses to_X() prefix for all date conversion functions
questions.correctAnswer: `year(col("date"))` — function names match almost 1:1 between SQL and PySpark for date operations
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: function-name parity. SQL `UPPER(name)` = PySpark `upper(col("name"))`. Same operations, almost-identical names. Fluency in one helps with the other.

questions.question: The principle "Learn both, specialize in one" suggests:
options: Write transformation pipelines in PySpark (your specialty); be COMPETENT in SQL for verification + ad-hoc queries
Avoid mixing — pick one language and use it for everything to keep the codebase consistent across the team
Specialize in SQL — PySpark is being deprecated by Microsoft in favor of full T-SQL coverage everywhere
Specialize in PySpark — SQL is for analysts only; data engineers should never write SQL professionally at all
questions.correctAnswer: Write transformation pipelines in PySpark (your specialty); be COMPETENT in SQL for verification + ad-hoc queries
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: specialty + competence. Daily PySpark for the heavy work. Weekly SQL for verification, communication with analysts, and quick stakeholder questions. Both languages are tools you reach for.

questions.question: NordGrid's analytical layer uses VIEWS extensively in the SQL endpoint. The benefit of views is:
options: Saved queries — analysts run views without rewriting GROUP BY/JOIN every time; engineers control the logic centrally
Speed — views pre-compute and cache results, making subsequent reads 10× faster than the underlying tables
Storage savings — views are stored in compressed binary format; the underlying tables can be deleted to save space
Required for Power BI — semantic models cannot reference Delta tables directly, only views
questions.correctAnswer: Saved queries — analysts run views without rewriting GROUP BY/JOIN every time; engineers control the logic centrally
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: views = the engineer-analyst contract. Engineer defines the canonical "monthly summary" view. Analysts query the view. If business logic changes, engineer updates the view; reports keep working.

questions.question: The medallion architecture has three layers. Each in ONE sentence:
options: Bronze = raw ingestion (no transformation). Silver = validated transformation. Gold = business-ready aggregates
Bronze = development. Silver = staging. Gold = production — the layers map to deployment environments
Bronze = small data. Silver = medium data. Gold = big data — the layers separate by data volume thresholds
Bronze = streaming. Silver = batch. Gold = real-time — the layers separate by data velocity types
questions.correctAnswer: Bronze = raw ingestion (no transformation). Silver = validated transformation. Gold = business-ready aggregates
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: each layer has ONE job. Bronze captures raw. Silver validates. Gold aggregates. Clear separation of concerns — each layer knows its job, doesn't do others'.

questions.question: Bronze stores raw data — but "raw doesn't mean disorganized." NordGrid's standard is:
options: Date-partitioned folders, consistent naming, source-attributed filenames — organized but unprocessed
A single flat folder with all files dumped together — true raw storage means zero human imposed structure
Files compressed with ZSTD before landing — Bronze is a write-once optimized read layer for archival
Bronze must be in Delta format — only Delta is supported in Fabric Lakehouses for any data
questions.correctAnswer: Date-partitioned folders, consistent naming, source-attributed filenames — organized but unprocessed
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: organized ≠ transformed. Bronze keeps the original bytes but in a structured location. Date-partitioned folders enable easy time-range backfills. Consistent naming makes the Bronze browsable.
