questions.question: Silver is "where the data engineering work happens." Why is Silver the most complex layer?
options: Type casting, null handling, deduplication, derived columns, schema enforcement, validation — all live here
Silver runs on dedicated hardware — performance tuning consumes most engineering time at this stage
Silver requires the most testing — quality issues at Silver propagate to Gold, so testing is most rigorous here
Silver tables are the largest — handling terabyte-scale tables requires custom optimization at every step
questions.correctAnswer: Type casting, null handling, deduplication, derived columns, schema enforcement, validation — all live here
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Silver = transformation logic. Bronze just stores raw bytes. Gold just aggregates. Silver does ALL the cleaning, validation, derivation — the meaningful engineering work.

questions.question: Gold tables are dramatically smaller than Silver tables. Example?
options: Silver = 500K rows/day. Gold daily summary = 4 rows/day (one per region). Aggregation collapses dramatically
Silver = 500K rows/day. Gold = 1M rows/day. Aggregation duplicates per consumer for performance
Silver = 500K rows/day. Gold = 100K rows/day. Aggregation reduces by 80% on average across pipelines
Silver = 500K rows/day. Gold = 500K rows/day. Aggregation does not change row count, only column count
questions.correctAnswer: Silver = 500K rows/day. Gold daily summary = 4 rows/day (one per region). Aggregation collapses dramatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Gold collapse. groupBy("region") reduces 500K rows to 4 (one per region). Power BI reports query 4 rows instantly. Silver retains full granularity for any drill-down need.

questions.question: Power BI reports connect to Lakehouse tables via Direct Lake. What does Direct Lake mean?
options: Power BI reads OneLake Delta files directly — no data import (always fresh) and no per-query database hits (fast)
Power BI imports the data into its own memory each morning — provides fast queries but stale data
Power BI queries Spark live for every chart — provides fresh data but slow rendering for users
Power BI uses a custom Microsoft format that's only readable by Power BI itself — Fabric writes this format
questions.correctAnswer: Power BI reads OneLake Delta files directly — no data import (always fresh) and no per-query database hits (fast)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: Direct Lake = fresh + fast. Import = fast but stale. DirectQuery = fresh but slow. Direct Lake reads Delta directly = both fresh AND fast. Unique to Fabric.

questions.question: Fabric's lineage view automatically tracks dependencies between FABRIC items. What does it NOT track?
options: Dependencies outside Fabric — external apps writing to OneLake, BI tools reading via APIs
Pipeline schedules — only tables and notebooks; pipelines are excluded from lineage
Spark cluster utilization — that requires the monitoring hub, not lineage view
User access patterns — Microsoft Purview tracks "who accessed what," not Fabric's lineage
questions.correctAnswer: Dependencies outside Fabric — external apps writing to OneLake, BI tools reading via APIs
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: lineage works WITHIN Fabric. External system writing to OneLake via ADLS Gen2 API doesn't register in lineage. Document those externally; don't rely on lineage to catch them all.

questions.question: NordGrid's morning routine catches "95% of overnight issues before anyone else notices." The first step is:
options: Open the monitoring hub, filter to overnight runs, scan for failed/long-running pipelines
Open Power BI and check that the morning dashboard renders correctly with fresh data
Run all unit tests on the codebase — verify pipeline integrity from code-level perspective
Email the platform team for status — daily standup with monitoring summary from infrastructure
questions.correctAnswer: Open the monitoring hub, filter to overnight runs, scan for failed/long-running pipelines
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: monitoring hub first. Two minutes to scan. Catches 95% of issues. Detect early → fix before stakeholders notice → minimize blast radius. Daily 5-minute discipline.

questions.question: Microsoft Purview's sensitivity labels (General/Confidential/Highly Confidential) are NOT just metadata. They:
options: TRIGGER enforcement policies — Confidential blocks export to personal email, Highly Confidential blocks unmanaged devices
Affect storage cost — labeled data is stored in encrypted tiers with different per-GB pricing
Determine query priority — Confidential queries always run before General queries on shared clusters
Set retention periods — General = 30 days, Confidential = 1 year, Highly Confidential = 7 years
questions.correctAnswer: TRIGGER enforcement policies — Confidential blocks export to personal email, Highly Confidential blocks unmanaged devices
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: labels are active. Apply "Confidential" to a table → users can't download to unmanaged devices. Apply "Highly Confidential" → DLP policies block external sharing. Real enforcement, not just classification.

questions.question: After completing this junior course, the next track is:
options: The mid-level Fabric track — builds on junior with topics like advanced security, optimization, and complex orchestration
The senior PySpark track — junior Fabric prepares you for advanced Spark internals at platform scale
A separate Power BI certification — Fabric junior content does not prepare for any further data engineering work
A migration to Databricks — Fabric is the prerequisite course for Databricks-specific data engineering
questions.correctAnswer: The mid-level Fabric track — builds on junior with topics like advanced security, optimization, and complex orchestration
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: vertical progression. Junior → Mid → Senior. Each track assumes the previous as foundation. The mid-level track explains "why each tool works the way it does and what happens when limits are reached."

questions.question: A data engineer's job is described as:
options: Making data USEFUL — tools (workspaces, Lakehouses, notebooks, pipelines, SQL) are means; useful data is the end
Mastering every Microsoft service — broad tool coverage is the metric of seniority for data engineers
Building dashboards — engineers must be fluent in Power BI to be fully effective in Fabric
Optimizing capacity costs — financial efficiency is the primary KPI for data engineering teams in Fabric
questions.correctAnswer: Making data USEFUL — tools (workspaces, Lakehouses, notebooks, pipelines, SQL) are means; useful data is the end
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: tools serve a purpose. The pipeline isn't the goal; reliable, queryable, well-documented data is. Master tools to deliver useful data — not for their own sake.

questions.question: NordGrid's mid-level performance discipline follows a four-step cycle. What's the right ORDER?
options: Measure → Diagnose → Fix → Verify — establish baseline first, then identify, then change, then re-test
Fix → Verify → Measure → Diagnose — apply a quick fix and check if it helped before deep investigation
Diagnose → Fix → Measure → Verify — start with the suspected cause, fix it, then measure the result
Verify → Measure → Diagnose → Fix — confirm SLA was breached, measure where, diagnose, then act
questions.correctAnswer: Measure → Diagnose → Fix → Verify — establish baseline first, then identify, then change, then re-test
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: order matters. Without a baseline, you can't prove the fix helped. Without diagnosis, you're guessing. Without verification, regressions slip through. The cycle prevents wasted optimization work.

questions.question: In a Fabric notebook, `df.explain()` prints the physical plan. How do you READ it?
options: Bottom-up — the lowest operator (FileScan) is where execution starts; the topmost is the final result
Top-down — the topmost operator runs first because Spark processes the plan in declared order
Left-to-right — operators on the left execute before operators on the right within each line
In random order — Catalyst can reorder operators at runtime so reading order is irrelevant entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Bottom-up — the lowest operator (FileScan) is where execution starts; the topmost is the final result
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: bottom-up. FileScan reads first, results bubble up through filters, joins, projects, to the final action at the top. Reading top-down inverts the data flow logic.

questions.question: A NordGrid query filters by `date` (a regular column) instead of `year, month` (the partition columns). The plan shows `PartitionFilters: []`. The result?
options: Spark scans EVERY partition, then filters in memory — much slower than partition pruning would have been
Catalyst auto-converts the filter to use partition columns, so performance is identical to using year/month
The query fails with an error — non-partition filters are not allowed on partitioned tables in Fabric
AQE detects the missing pruning at runtime and skips non-matching partitions before reading anything
questions.correctAnswer: Spark scans EVERY partition, then filters in memory — much slower than partition pruning would have been
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: partition pruning needs the partition columns. Filtering by `date` only checks each row after reading. Empty PartitionFilters in the plan = full scan = slow. Add year/month filters to enable pruning.

questions.question: NordGrid's Silver table has 9 columns. A regional summary uses 2. The plan should show `ReadSchema` with:
options: Just the 2 needed columns — Parquet's columnar layout lets Spark skip reading the other 7 entirely
All 9 columns — Spark reads everything and discards unused columns at the projection step
The 2 needed columns + the partition column — partitioning forces partition columns to always appear
A header-only row — actual data is loaded lazily as filters narrow down which columns are needed
questions.correctAnswer: Just the 2 needed columns — Parquet's columnar layout lets Spark skip reading the other 7 entirely
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Parquet is columnar. ReadSchema reflects what's actually read from disk. If your query selects 2 columns, ReadSchema should show 2 — not 9. Otherwise column pruning isn't working.

questions.question: Why does NordGrid sometimes lower `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` from the default 200 below 200?
options: When the shuffle output is small (e.g. 12 region rows after groupBy), 200 partitions creates excessive task overhead
Lower partition counts always reduce shuffle volume; 200 is a deprecated legacy default in newer Fabric versions
Spark requires partition counts to be powers of 2; 200 is not optimal under any data condition
Lower partition counts trigger Catalyst's broadcast optimization automatically, regardless of table sizes
questions.correctAnswer: When the shuffle output is small (e.g. 12 region rows after groupBy), 200 partitions creates excessive task overhead
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: target = 128-256MB per partition. groupBy('region') on 12 regions produces 12 output rows — 200 tasks for 12 rows is scheduling overhead, not parallelism. AQE often handles this automatically.

questions.question: The Spark UI is the "performance debugger" — what does it show that `explain()` does NOT?
options: ACTUAL runtime metrics — task durations, real row counts, shuffle volumes, GC time, executor utilization
The plan tree — explain() shows code-level operators while Spark UI shows the execution graph at runtime
The output rows — the SQL/DataFrame tab is the only way to inspect query results in Fabric notebooks
Cluster cost — the Spark UI is the source of CU consumption metrics for each notebook execution
questions.correctAnswer: ACTUAL runtime metrics — task durations, real row counts, shuffle volumes, GC time, executor utilization
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: explain = intent (what Spark plans to do). Spark UI = reality (what actually happened — including how long, how much data, where it skewed). Both together diagnose performance.

questions.question: AQE's 3 features — coalesce partitions, convert sort-merge to broadcast, skew handling — share what trait?
options: They all use ACTUAL runtime stats (post-shuffle data size) instead of compile-time estimates
They all run only at the very end of a query — too late to affect that run, but cached for the next
They all require explicit hints in code; AQE is opt-in per query rather than enabled by default
They all replace Catalyst entirely once enabled, so rule-based optimizations no longer apply
questions.correctAnswer: They all use ACTUAL runtime stats (post-shuffle data size) instead of compile-time estimates
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: Catalyst guesses at compile time. AQE measures at runtime. Once a shuffle is done, AQE knows exactly how much data is on each partition — and can re-decide partition count, join strategy, or skew splitting.

questions.question: NordGrid's 47-minute notebook had ONE dominant bottleneck consuming 80%+ of the time. The lesson is:
options: In 80% of slow notebooks, a single bottleneck causes most of the runtime — find it, fix it, dramatic gains
Slow notebooks have many small bottlenecks; addressing them all is required for any meaningful improvement
Slow notebooks are caused by Spark version regressions; the fix is always upgrading to the latest Spark
Slow notebooks usually have hardware bottlenecks; the fix is always to scale up the cluster's capacity SKU
questions.correctAnswer: In 80% of slow notebooks, a single bottleneck causes most of the runtime — find it, fix it, dramatic gains
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 80/20 rule. Most slow notebooks have one dominant problem (huge shuffle, missing pruning, skew). Diagnose to find that one, fix it, get a 3-5× improvement. Don't waste time on minor issues until the big one is solved.

questions.question: "Capacity throttling" in Fabric happens when:
options: Multiple workloads (notebooks, Power BI refresh, Warehouse queries) consume more CUs than the capacity pool allows
A notebook runs for more than 60 minutes; Fabric automatically kills any session that long
The Spark version is older than 3.5; Fabric throttles old versions to encourage upgrades
A user runs more than 10 queries in an hour; Fabric throttles individual users for fair sharing
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple workloads (notebooks, Power BI refresh, Warehouse queries) consume more CUs than the capacity pool allows
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: shared capacity = competition. When everyone runs at once, throttling slows lower-priority work. NordGrid staggers Silver (02:00), Gold (02:20), Power BI refresh (04:00) to avoid overlap.

questions.question: NordGrid's "20% headroom rule" means:
options: When pipeline duration is within 80% of the SLA, take optimization action before SLAs are at risk
Capacity is purchased with 20% over-provisioning to handle unexpected workload spikes
Engineers can spend 20% of their time on performance work without explicit project allocation
Performance issues are escalated only when affecting more than 20% of consumer queries
questions.correctAnswer: When pipeline duration is within 80% of the SLA, take optimization action before SLAs are at risk
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: act before crisis. SLA is 20 minutes? Headroom triggers when pipeline runs 16+ minutes. Catches gradual degradation before consumers experience late data.

questions.question: The junior-to-mid Delta transition is described as "from write-and-forget to write-and-maintain." What changes?
options: Mid-level adds operations like MERGE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, schema evolution — ongoing maintenance, not just writes
Mid-level abandons Delta in favor of Iceberg, which has better governance for multi-team use
Mid-level requires manual transaction log management; Delta's automatic logging is disabled at scale
Mid-level moves all storage to Azure Data Lake Storage; Delta is deprecated in Fabric mid-level pipelines
questions.correctAnswer: Mid-level adds operations like MERGE, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, schema evolution — ongoing maintenance, not just writes
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: writes are necessary, maintenance is required. MERGE for incremental updates. OPTIMIZE for compaction. VACUUM for cleanup. Schema evolution for change. The engineer's job extends past `saveAsTable`.

questions.question: NordGrid switched from `mode("overwrite")` to MERGE. Why?
options: At 1.3 billion rows, full overwrite became too slow; MERGE only rewrites files containing changed rows
Overwrite was deprecated in Delta 3.0; MERGE became the only supported write method
MERGE consumes fewer CUs than overwrite for any data size; it is always the better choice
MERGE is the only operation that supports schema evolution; overwrite always rejects new columns
questions.correctAnswer: At 1.3 billion rows, full overwrite became too slow; MERGE only rewrites files containing changed rows
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: scale-driven decision. Overwrite at 500K rows = 8 minutes. At 1.3 billion = 42 minutes. MERGE only touches the changed parts — incremental update at large scale.

questions.question: NordGrid's first MERGE took 38 MINUTES — longer than the overwrite it replaced. The cause was:
options: Missing partition filter in the match condition — MERGE scanned every partition looking for matches
Selective updates were not enabled, so MERGE rewrote every row even when values were identical
The source DataFrame was uncached, forcing recomputation during the MERGE's join phase
AQE was disabled for the workspace, preventing skew handling on the join key column
questions.correctAnswer: Missing partition filter in the match condition — MERGE scanned every partition looking for matches
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: MERGE without `target.year = source.year AND target.month = source.month` reads ALL partitions to look for matches. With the partition filter, only the relevant partition is scanned. Massive performance difference.

questions.question: Adding a NEW COLUMN to a Delta table requires which option?
options: `mergeSchema=true` — additively merges the new column without rewriting existing data
`overwriteSchema=true` — replaces the entire schema, rewriting all historical data with the new schema
`alterTable=true` — Fabric requires explicit ALTER TABLE before any schema change can apply
A platform-team approval ticket — schema changes are blocked at the workspace level for safety reasons
questions.correctAnswer: `mergeSchema=true` — additively merges the new column without rewriting existing data
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: mergeSchema = additive (safe, fast, no rewrite). overwriteSchema = breaking (destructive, slow, rewrites everything). For new columns, you want mergeSchema.

questions.question: OPTIMIZE compacts small files into larger ones. The KEY benefit is:
options: Fewer files = fewer per-file open operations during reads — significant speedup for queries on fragmented tables
Smaller storage footprint — OPTIMIZE compresses data more aggressively than the original write
Better security — fewer files reduce the surface area for access control violations
Faster writes — subsequent writes to the table use the compacted layout for better throughput
questions.correctAnswer: Fewer files = fewer per-file open operations during reads — significant speedup for queries on fragmented tables
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: each Parquet file = an open + footer-read + decode. Hundreds of small files = hundreds of these overheads. OPTIMIZE merges to ~1GB files = much fewer overhead operations.

questions.question: NordGrid runs OPTIMIZE BEFORE VACUUM, never the reverse. Why?
options: OPTIMIZE creates new files and supersedes old ones; VACUUM only deletes superseded files — order matters
VACUUM corrupts the transaction log; OPTIMIZE must run first to repair the log before VACUUM
OPTIMIZE is much slower than VACUUM; running OPTIMIZE first frees capacity for VACUUM's expensive scan
They can run in either order; the convention is for monitoring dashboard layout consistency only
questions.correctAnswer: OPTIMIZE creates new files and supersedes old ones; VACUUM only deletes superseded files — order matters
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: dependencies. OPTIMIZE writes new compacted files and marks old as superseded. VACUUM then physically deletes superseded files. Reverse order = VACUUM has nothing to delete; OPTIMIZE then creates new files immediately superseded.

questions.question: NordGrid's `RETAIN 336 HOURS` (14 days) on VACUUM means:
options: Files referenced by versions newer than 14 days are kept; older superseded files are physically deleted
The Delta transaction log is truncated to keep only the last 14 days of history
Time-travel queries are limited to 14 days regardless of file retention; older queries always fail
VACUUM runs every 14 days — the retention parameter controls the schedule frequency for the operation
questions.correctAnswer: Files referenced by versions newer than 14 days are kept; older superseded files are physically deleted
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: retention bounds time-travel. 14 days = you can query the table as of any version within the last 14 days; older versions' files are gone. Default minimum is 7 days; lower triggers a safety check.

questions.question: For high-frequency streaming writes that produce many small files, NordGrid uses:
options: `optimizeWrite=true` — coalesces small partitions during writes BEFORE files are created
A nightly OPTIMIZE job — the most common pattern for any streaming workload regardless of frequency
Manual file deletion via Azure Storage Explorer — small files are removed by automation outside Spark
A separate Lakehouse for streaming — file fragmentation cannot be prevented in shared Lakehouses
questions.correctAnswer: `optimizeWrite=true` — coalesces small partitions during writes BEFORE files are created
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: prevention vs cure. optimizeWrite consolidates output partitions during the write itself, preventing small files at the source. OPTIMIZE then compacts what gets through. Both work together.

questions.question: Time travel after a production incident — query the table at version BEFORE the incident:
options: `spark.read.option("versionAsOf", N)` — read the table's state at exactly that version
`spark.read.option("snapshotId", N)` — Delta's snapshot mechanism for incident response
`spark.read.history(N)` — fetch all rows that existed at version N
`spark.sql("RESTORE TABLE TO VERSION N")` — must be the first step before any read operation
questions.correctAnswer: `spark.read.option("versionAsOf", N)` — read the table's state at exactly that version
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: simple read with version option. Compare current vs versionAsOf to quantify the incident's impact. Then RESTORE if needed to revert. Time travel is non-destructive — you can always read history.

questions.question: A Delta change at one layer ripples through what?
options: Three layers — the Delta table, the SQL endpoint metadata, and the semantic model in Power BI
Just the table itself — Fabric's SQL endpoint and semantic model are independently configured
Just the workspace where the change happens — other workspaces require explicit propagation
Just the consumers who explicitly subscribed to change notifications via Microsoft Purview
questions.correctAnswer: Three layers — the Delta table, the SQL endpoint metadata, and the semantic model in Power BI
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: schema changes are cross-layer events. Delta table updates → SQL endpoint detects within seconds → semantic model needs explicit refresh. Plan changes considering the cascade.

questions.question: Junior pipelines were linear (3 activities). Mid pipelines need WHAT new patterns?
options: Error handling, conditional branching, ForEach loops, parent-child orchestration, dynamic configuration
Visual canvas — junior pipelines used code; mid pipelines use the visual designer instead
A different orchestrator — Fabric mid-level requires Apache Airflow instead of Data Factory pipelines
A bigger Spark cluster — pattern complexity is purely about scaling compute resources
questions.correctAnswer: Error handling, conditional branching, ForEach loops, parent-child orchestration, dynamic configuration
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: more regions, more retries, more dependencies → more pipeline patterns. Linear breaks at scale; resilience requires retry, branching, looping, and decomposition.

questions.question: A "TRANSIENT failure" benefits from retry; a "PERMANENT failure" does NOT. Example of permanent?
options: The vendor didn't generate today's file — retrying every 5 minutes wastes 15 minutes for nothing
The SFTP server is unreachable — usually a brief network blip that resolves on retry
A Spark executor lost its connection — common transient failure that retries handle automatically
A 503 from a REST API — typically the server is overloaded and retry usually succeeds
questions.correctAnswer: The vendor didn't generate today's file — retrying every 5 minutes wastes 15 minutes for nothing
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: transient = the cause goes away by itself. Permanent = the cause requires human action. Vendor missing file is permanent until they generate it. SFTP blip is transient until the network recovers.

questions.question: NordGrid's "If Condition" activity uses a Lookup activity first to check the row count. The pattern is:
options: Lookup the file metadata, branch on the result — skip transformation if the file is empty (vendor export error)
Lookup the SQL endpoint version, branch on whether to refresh — automatic cache invalidation
Lookup user permissions, branch on whether to run — security gate before transformation
Lookup the cluster's available capacity, branch on whether to defer — cost optimization gate
questions.correctAnswer: Lookup the file metadata, branch on the result — skip transformation if the file is empty (vendor export error)
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: defensive pipeline. Empty Bronze file = vendor problem. Don't run the transformation; alert and skip. Saves capacity and prevents downstream confusion from empty Silver tables.

questions.question: A ForEach loop with `isSequential=false` and `batchCount=4` means:
options: Iterations run in parallel, but maximum 4 at a time — balances parallelism with capacity consumption
All 12 iterations run simultaneously; batchCount is informational metadata only for monitoring
Iterations run sequentially in batches of 4 — each batch starts only after the previous completes
Iterations run on 4 different Spark clusters; batchCount controls cluster allocation
questions.correctAnswer: Iterations run in parallel, but maximum 4 at a time — balances parallelism with capacity consumption
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: parallelism cap. 12 regions × parallel = capacity overload. Sequential = too slow (12× the duration). batchCount=4 = 4 concurrent at a time, processed in waves until done.

questions.question: NordGrid stores pipeline configuration in a CONFIG TABLE rather than pipeline parameters. Why?
options: Changing config doesn't require editing the pipeline; multiple environments share one pipeline definition
Pipeline parameters are limited to 256 characters; tables hold longer JSON configurations effectively
Tables are queryable by analysts; pipeline parameters are visible only to engineers with edit access
Required by Fabric — pipelines with more than 5 parameters must externalize them to a table by policy
questions.correctAnswer: Changing config doesn't require editing the pipeline; multiple environments share one pipeline definition
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: separation of code and config. Pipeline = behavior. Config table = parameters. dev/staging/prod read different rows from the same table. Single pipeline definition, three behaviors.

questions.question: NordGrid uses parent-child decomposition when a pipeline grows past 10 activities. Why?
options: A 20-activity pipeline is hard to read, debug, and maintain; decomposing produces smaller focused units
Fabric enforces a 10-activity limit per pipeline; larger pipelines are rejected by the platform
The Spark optimizer cannot handle DAGs with more than 10 activities efficiently at runtime
Larger pipelines fail more often than smaller ones; decomposing improves reliability via isolation
questions.correctAnswer: A 20-activity pipeline is hard to read, debug, and maintain; decomposing produces smaller focused units
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: cognitive load. 20 activities in one canvas is a wall of arrows. Decomposing into Bronze child + Silver child + Gold child = 3 focused pipelines. Each understandable. Easier to test, monitor, and modify.

questions.question: NordGrid's "dual-mode pipeline" runs DAILY mode (one date) or BACKFILL mode (date range). The KEY requirement?
options: IDEMPOTENCY — re-processing a date must produce the same result as processing it once
Sequential execution — backfills must process dates in chronological order strictly
Manual approval per date — backfills require explicit operator confirmation for each date
Separate pipelines — backfill and daily must be distinct DAGs in different workspaces
questions.correctAnswer: IDEMPOTENCY — re-processing a date must produce the same result as processing it once
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: idempotent = repeatable. MERGE handles this naturally (re-merging same data = no duplicates). Append-only writes need explicit dedup or replaceWhere logic.

questions.question: NordGrid's `pipeline_metrics` table records per-run metadata. Why write it during the pipeline?
options: Enables proactive monitoring (trend analysis, SLA tracking, regression detection) beyond reactive checks
Required by Fabric — every pipeline run must write a metrics record for billing audit purposes
Provides backup data — if the actual data is corrupted, metrics can be used to reconstruct it
Powers Power BI reports — analysts need detailed pipeline metrics for their dashboards
questions.correctAnswer: Enables proactive monitoring (trend analysis, SLA tracking, regression detection) beyond reactive checks
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: metrics turn ops into engineering. Without them, you only react to failures. With them, you see "average duration trending up over 4 weeks" before SLA breaches hit.

questions.question: A "DRY RUN" pipeline parameter validates the configuration WITHOUT executing data operations. The benefit?
options: Catch broken expressions, missing connections, and config typos in seconds — before a real run wastes capacity
Saves storage by not writing actual output — every dry run skips the write phase entirely
Required for compliance — production pipelines must dry-run before each real run for audit trail
Faster than real runs — dry mode runs on dedicated low-priority capacity to save normal CUs
questions.correctAnswer: Catch broken expressions, missing connections, and config typos in seconds — before a real run wastes capacity
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: pipeline lint. Dry run = "would this work?" without doing the work. Catches typos in expressions, missing parameters, and bad references before you spend 30 minutes running a doomed pipeline.

questions.question: NordGrid's pipeline tracks 23 failures over 6 months. The proper response is:
options: Failures are normal at this scale — every failure produces a post-mortem and prevents the next occurrence
Replace the pipeline — 23 failures indicates fundamental design issues with the architecture
Add more retries everywhere — most failures are transient and could be hidden by aggressive retry
Reduce SLA targets — the team is over-promising; relaxing SLAs eliminates failure events
questions.correctAnswer: Failures are normal at this scale — every failure produces a post-mortem and prevents the next occurrence
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: failures aren't shame. Each failure = data quality / capacity / dependency issue. Post-mortem = "what specifically prevents this from happening again." 23 failures is 23 lessons learned.

questions.question: In the Fabric Warehouse, table DISTRIBUTION strategies determine:
options: How rows are spread across compute nodes — affects whether joins can be local or require shuffle
How tables are encrypted at rest — controls per-row vs per-column encryption granularity
How frequently statistics are updated — distribution choice triggers different refresh schedules
How indexes are stored on disk — distribution maps to physical index layout in the Warehouse
questions.correctAnswer: How rows are spread across compute nodes — affects whether joins can be local or require shuffle
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: distribution = node placement. Hash distribution on the join key keeps matching rows together. Round-robin spreads evenly without considering joins. Choice matters for query performance.

questions.question: NordGrid's query joined a 90M-row Silver table with a 12-row dim_regions table. It took 3 hours instead of seconds. Why?
options: Stale statistics — the optimizer estimated dim_regions at 500K rows and chose nested-loop instead of broadcast
Network bottleneck — cross-database queries are inherently slower than single-database queries
Missing indexes on dim_regions — the Warehouse cannot join without indexes on the smaller table
AQE was disabled — without it, the optimizer cannot detect small tables at runtime to broadcast them
questions.correctAnswer: Stale statistics — the optimizer estimated dim_regions at 500K rows and chose nested-loop instead of broadcast
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: stale stats lie. Optimizer thought dim_regions was big → no broadcast → nested loop on 90M rows = disaster. UPDATE STATISTICS fixed it: estimated 12 rows → broadcast → done in seconds.

questions.question: A MATERIALIZED VIEW differs from a regular view by:
options: Pre-computing and STORING the result; queries that match the view's pattern serve from the cache, not the base tables
Being writable (you can INSERT into materialized views); regular views are always read-only
Supporting joins (regular views cannot join across tables); materialized views unlock cross-table operations
Being faster to create; materialized views skip schema validation that regular views perform on creation
questions.correctAnswer: Pre-computing and STORING the result; queries that match the view's pattern serve from the cache, not the base tables
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: regular view = saved query (re-runs every time). Materialized view = saved result (returned directly when matching queries hit). Faster reads, but needs refresh after base table changes.

questions.question: NordGrid's STORED PROCEDURES use TRY/CATCH with `THROW` in the catch block. Why is THROW essential?
options: Without THROW, the procedure swallows the error and reports success — pipelines never know about failures
Required by SQL Server's syntax — TRY/CATCH blocks must always end with THROW to be well-formed
For audit logging — THROW writes a log entry that the platform team uses for compliance reporting
Performance — THROW signals the optimizer to skip remaining cleanup, making the catch block faster
questions.correctAnswer: Without THROW, the procedure swallows the error and reports success — pipelines never know about failures
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: silent failure pattern. CATCH without THROW = error logged + procedure returns "success" → pipeline thinks it worked → bad data flows downstream. THROW re-raises so the caller knows.

questions.question: The Warehouse's "unified query layer" uses VIEWS that join tables across multiple Lakehouses. The benefit?
options: Analysts query simple views; engineers can change underlying joins/sources without breaking analyst code
Eliminates data copying — views run cross-database queries instead of materializing into the Warehouse
Required by SOX compliance — financial data must always be queried through views, never tables
Speeds up queries — views are 10× faster than the underlying tables they query in any scenario
questions.correctAnswer: Analysts query simple views; engineers can change underlying joins/sources without breaking analyst code
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: views as APIs. "v_monthly_summary" is the analyst contract. Engineer can rewrite the query to add a new source, optimize the join, or move data. Analysts unaffected. The view name stays.

questions.question: T-SQL window functions answer "how does each row relate to its group?" — distinct from GROUP BY because:
options: They preserve every row of input — adding a computed column instead of collapsing into one row per group
They run faster than GROUP BY at any scale because they skip the aggregation step entirely
They support more functions than GROUP BY (any function can be windowed; only some can be grouped)
They are write operations; GROUP BY is read-only and cannot modify the underlying tables
questions.correctAnswer: They preserve every row of input — adding a computed column instead of collapsing into one row per group
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: GROUP BY = "total per region" (collapses). Window function = "this row's ranking within its region" (preserves all rows). Different semantics, different use cases.

questions.question: NordGrid: "60% of slow Warehouse queries are fixed by updating statistics." Why is this so common?
options: Stats become stale silently as data grows; the optimizer makes bad decisions based on outdated row counts
Statistics are mandatory but rarely run — most teams skip them until performance issues force the refresh
Statistics replace the query plan entirely; updating them generates better plans automatically
Statistics are encrypted; updating them re-encrypts the table data and improves storage layout
questions.correctAnswer: Stats become stale silently as data grows; the optimizer makes bad decisions based on outdated row counts
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: stale stats = silent slow queries. UPDATE STATISTICS is the cheapest performance fix. NordGrid runs it weekly on all tables. Catches degradation before it manifests as user complaints.

questions.question: Row-Level Security (RLS) in the Warehouse attaches a security predicate to a table. The KEY caveat?
options: It applies to Warehouse queries ONLY — direct access to the underlying Lakehouse SQL endpoint bypasses RLS
It applies only to SELECT — INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE always see all rows regardless of policy
It applies only to authenticated users — anonymous queries automatically bypass all RLS rules
It applies once per session — the predicate is evaluated only when the user first connects to the Warehouse
questions.correctAnswer: It applies to Warehouse queries ONLY — direct access to the underlying Lakehouse SQL endpoint bypasses RLS
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: RLS is at the Warehouse layer. If the Silver Lakehouse SQL endpoint is also accessible to the same users, RLS doesn't protect there. Solution: restrict Lakehouse SQL endpoint access to engineering only.

questions.question: NordGrid's Gold Warehouse architecture has 8 tables, 2 materialized views, 3 stored procedures. The pattern is:
options: Tables hold facts/dimensions, materialized views pre-compute hot queries, stored procedures encapsulate refresh logic
Tables are the analyst interface; views are deprecated; stored procedures replaced with notebooks
Each layer is encrypted differently; the count reflects different security tiers per item type
Tables, views, and procedures are 1:1; the Warehouse limits how many of each type can be created
questions.correctAnswer: Tables hold facts/dimensions, materialized views pre-compute hot queries, stored procedures encapsulate refresh logic
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: separation of concerns. Tables = data. Materialized views = optimized read access. Stored procedures = "how to refresh." Each piece has a clear job in the Warehouse architecture.

questions.question: The Warehouse is described as the "platform's front door" because:
options: Consumers interact through the Warehouse — they don't see Bronze, Silver notebooks, or Gold pipelines
It is the only item type Fabric advertises to external users; other items are hidden by default
It runs at the perimeter of the platform; firewalls protect everything else from external access
It generates the platform's primary revenue; other items are loss leaders for the Warehouse business
questions.correctAnswer: Consumers interact through the Warehouse — they don't see Bronze, Silver notebooks, or Gold pipelines
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: consumers see only the front door. The engineering work (transformations, pipelines, ingestion) is invisible. The Warehouse / SQL endpoint = where everyone meets the data.

questions.question: Real-time processing in NordGrid's architecture COMPLEMENTS the daily batch pipeline rather than REPLACING it. Why?
options: Different needs — meter readings (daily ok) use batch; turbine telemetry (sub-second) uses real-time
Real-time pipelines are inherently unstable; batch is the safety net when streams fail
Real-time has 10× higher CU cost; running both is required to balance compute consumption
Compliance requires both — regulators audit both batch and stream paths for any production data
questions.correctAnswer: Different needs — meter readings (daily ok) use batch; turbine telemetry (sub-second) uses real-time
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: data velocity decides path. Daily reports = batch. Operational alerts = real-time. Same data architecture; different pipelines for different latency needs.

questions.question: NordGrid's Real-Time Intelligence uses 4 components. Match the analogy: Eventstream is to ___ as a Copy activity is to ___.
options: Eventstream is to streaming what Copy activity is to batch — both move data from source to destination
Eventstream is to KQL what Copy activity is to PySpark — language-specific data transfer
Eventstream is to Eventhouse what Copy activity is to Lakehouse — both internal storage transitions
Eventstream is to dashboards what Copy activity is to reports — direct visualization paths
questions.correctAnswer: Eventstream is to streaming what Copy activity is to batch — both move data from source to destination
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: parallel architecture. Both ingest data into Fabric, just different velocity tiers. Eventstream = continuous from Event Hubs/Kafka; Copy = batch from SFTP/SQL.

questions.question: KQL Eventhouse vs Delta Lakehouse — what's the KEY difference?
options: KQL is optimized for high-frequency time-series queries; Delta is optimized for batch analytical reads
KQL stores data on disk only; Delta is in-memory only — different storage tiers entirely
KQL supports T-SQL only; Delta supports PySpark and SQL — language coverage differs
KQL is read-write; Delta is read-only — write capability is the dividing factor
questions.correctAnswer: KQL is optimized for high-frequency time-series queries; Delta is optimized for batch analytical reads
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: KQL is purpose-built. Streaming events arrive constantly → KQL ingests fast and indexes by time. Delta is batch-optimized for periodic large writes and analytical queries.

questions.question: KQL's "pipe-based model" (`StagingTable | where x > 0 | summarize ...`) is similar to:
options: PySpark method chaining or Unix pipes — data flows through a chain of operators left-to-right
SQL JOIN syntax — operators are joined together via shared columns in fixed positions
Object inheritance — each operator inherits properties from the previous operator
Recursion — operators call themselves until a termination condition is reached
questions.correctAnswer: PySpark method chaining or Unix pipes — data flows through a chain of operators left-to-right
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: same mental model as `df.filter(...).groupBy(...).agg(...)`. KQL's syntax just uses `|` instead of `.`. Function names differ but the dataflow concept is identical.

questions.question: Why does NordGrid route Eventstream data to BOTH Bronze Lakehouse AND KQL database?
options: KQL serves real-time dashboards; Lakehouse Bronze enables Silver consolidation and historical analysis
KQL is unreliable; Lakehouse is the durable backup that survives Eventstream failures
KQL only retains 7 days; Lakehouse Bronze retains data forever for audit
They are functionally identical; routing to both is redundant but required for SOX compliance
questions.correctAnswer: KQL serves real-time dashboards; Lakehouse Bronze enables Silver consolidation and historical analysis
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: each store has a purpose. KQL = "what just happened?" (live dashboards). Lakehouse Bronze = "what happened over time?" (downstream batch analysis). Same data, two purposes.

questions.question: NordGrid's real-time dashboard refreshes every 30 seconds. Each dashboard query MUST complete in:
options: Under 2 seconds — about 10% of the refresh interval; queries slower than this stack up under load
Under 30 seconds — exactly the refresh interval; longer queries miss their refresh slot
Under 1 minute — twice the refresh interval; KQL automatically queues slow queries for the next refresh
Any duration — the dashboard waits as long as needed to show fresh data correctly
questions.correctAnswer: Under 2 seconds — about 10% of the refresh interval; queries slower than this stack up under load
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 10% rule. With 30s refresh, a 5s query consumes too much time and queues build up. 2s queries leave time for retries, network, rendering. Design queries within the budget.

questions.question: Why does NordGrid track "ingestion lag" as a streaming metric?
options: Tracks how fresh the KQL data is relative to source — high lag means consumers see stale "real-time" data
Tracks how often the Eventstream restarts — high lag indicates infrastructure instability
Tracks billing — Eventstream consumption is metered by lag duration in Fabric
Tracks user load — lag rises when too many users query the same Eventhouse simultaneously
questions.correctAnswer: Tracks how fresh the KQL data is relative to source — high lag means consumers see stale "real-time" data
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: lag = data freshness. "Real-time" with 5-min lag isn't real-time anymore. Tracking lag and alerting on threshold breaches catches degradation before consumers notice stale dashboards.

questions.question: NordGrid's real-time architecture is described as: "the batch path is the safety net." This means:
options: If real-time fails, the next morning's batch processes the same data and reconciles — no data is lost
Batch and real-time always run on identical data; one is just a backup of the other for failover
Real-time is for development; batch is production — only batch is treated as authoritative
Real-time hands off to batch every hour; batch processes the accumulated stream data periodically
questions.correctAnswer: If real-time fails, the next morning's batch processes the same data and reconciles — no data is lost
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: defense in depth. Stream goes down for 3 hours? The next batch run picks up the same Bronze data and completes the analytical pipeline. Live operational view loses 3 hours; nothing is permanently lost.

questions.question: NordGrid's production real-time pipeline processes 43 million events per day. The dashboard P95 latency is:
options: 400 milliseconds — well within the 2-second budget for 30-second refresh intervals
15 seconds — at the edge of the budget; close monitoring required to catch degradation
90 seconds — too slow; the dashboard is configured for 5-minute refreshes only
10 minutes — only acceptable for batch dashboards; real-time targets are not met
questions.correctAnswer: 400 milliseconds — well within the 2-second budget for 30-second refresh intervals
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: real numbers. 400ms is fast enough that the dashboard rendering (network + browser) dominates. KQL's columnar + indexing handles 43M events/day with sub-second queries.

questions.question: Real-time intelligence is a "new skill set" because:
options: KQL, Eventstream, Eventhouse, Real-Time Dashboard — entirely different vocabulary from PySpark/Delta
Engineers need a new certification per Microsoft policy before working on real-time pipelines
Real-time uses different cloud storage; existing OneLake skills don't transfer to the streaming layer
Real-time requires switching to Linux from Windows; the development environment shifts entirely
questions.correctAnswer: KQL, Eventstream, Eventhouse, Real-Time Dashboard — entirely different vocabulary from PySpark/Delta
questions.explanation: Lesson 1 (re-emphasized in 10): batch skills (PySpark, Delta, T-SQL) don't cover real-time. New tools, new query language, new architectural patterns. Plan training time before adoption.

questions.question: NordGrid's "deduplication bug" was caught by which testing layer?
options: Unit tests — would catch transformation logic flaws BEFORE production. Validation cells caught it AFTER
Integration tests caught it during staging promotion; unit tests would not have detected the issue
Schema tests caught it because the output schema differed; logic tests would have missed it
Production monitoring caught it via row count anomalies — unit and integration tests had no visibility
questions.correctAnswer: Unit tests — would catch transformation logic flaws BEFORE production. Validation cells caught it AFTER
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: testing pyramid. Unit tests (cheap, fast, catch logic bugs early) at the base. Validation = last line of defense. Without unit tests, bugs only surface after data is already wrong.

questions.question: A unit test for a Fabric notebook transformation requires:
options: EXTRACTING the transformation logic into standalone Python functions that take/return DataFrames
Running the entire notebook against production data; unit tests validate the full transformation context
A separate testing-only Fabric workspace; tests cannot run in development workspaces
A specific testing framework Microsoft provides; pytest is not supported in Fabric notebooks
questions.correctAnswer: EXTRACTING the transformation logic into standalone Python functions that take/return DataFrames
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: testable code = pure functions. Notebook = function calls + infrastructure. Extract function = test it in isolation with controlled inputs. Then notebook = thin wrapper.

questions.question: NordGrid's test suite has 45 cases — 5 happy path, 40 EDGE CASES. Why this ratio?
options: Edge cases (empty, all-null, duplicates, missing columns) are where bugs hide; happy path rarely fails
Edge cases are mandatory by Fabric testing standards; happy path is optional documentation only
Edge cases test the SQL endpoint; happy path tests the Spark side — both layers are required
Edge cases run faster than happy path; the ratio reflects test execution time optimization only
questions.correctAnswer: Edge cases (empty, all-null, duplicates, missing columns) are where bugs hide; happy path rarely fails
questions.explanation: Lesson 2 closing: 5 happy path tests prove the function works. 40 edge case tests prove it survives REAL production data. Each edge case is a bug that already happened or will happen.

questions.question: Test data should be MINIMAL (3-10 rows) but COMPLETE. Why this trade-off?
options: Small data is fast to create and inspect; complete coverage means each row tests a specific behavior
Spark requires test data to be exactly 7 rows; smaller fails initialization, larger fails optimization
Test data is billed per row; minimal data is the cost-optimization decision for test capacity
Test data must match production schema exactly; 10 rows is the minimum to fit all columns
questions.correctAnswer: Small data is fast to create and inspect; complete coverage means each row tests a specific behavior
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: each row earns its place. Row 1 tests valid input. Row 2 tests null. Row 3 tests boundary value. Etc. 10 rows of intentional test data > 1000 rows of accidental production sample.

questions.question: A SCHEMA VALIDATION test catches what?
options: Drift — when transformation output differs from the documented expected schema (column added, type changed)
Logic bugs — wrong values in correct columns; schema tests are about structure not values
Permission issues — unauthorized writes are caught at the schema layer for compliance reasons
Performance regressions — schema tests track query plan changes that signal slowdowns
questions.correctAnswer: Drift — when transformation output differs from the documented expected schema (column added, type changed)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: schema = structural contract. Test ensures output matches contract. Without schema tests, mergeSchema silently adds columns to production tables — surprise!

questions.question: A "data quality framework" (like Great Expectations) verifies WHAT?
options: Quantitative properties — row counts within range, null rates below threshold, value distributions in bounds
Code quality — function complexity, line count, code duplication metrics for the transformation
Schema correctness — type conformance and column presence; same purpose as schema validation tests
Pipeline orchestration — activity sequence and dependency correctness in the Data Factory canvas
questions.correctAnswer: Quantitative properties — row counts within range, null rates below threshold, value distributions in bounds
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: data quality = expectations on the data. "daily_kw between 0 and 5000". "<5% nulls in region". When violated, the test fails. Codifies what "correct data" means for downstream consumers.

questions.question: INTEGRATION tests differ from UNIT tests because they:
options: Run the entire pipeline end-to-end on a representative dataset — catch issues at stage boundaries
Run faster than unit tests; integration tests skip the most expensive verifications by design
Run against production data; unit tests are limited to synthetic test fixtures only
Run in CI only; unit tests run locally during development on engineer machines
questions.correctAnswer: Run the entire pipeline end-to-end on a representative dataset — catch issues at stage boundaries
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: unit tests = single function in isolation. Integration tests = the whole pipeline (read → transform → write). Catches "function works alone, fails when chained." Schema mismatches between stages, etc.

questions.question: A "GOLDEN DATASET" regression test compares:
options: Current output against a known-good frozen output for the same input — any difference fails the test
Performance metrics across releases; any slowdown vs the golden run fails the test
Schema between staging and production; any mismatch fails the test
Encryption strength; the golden hash is the baseline for security verification
questions.correctAnswer: Current output against a known-good frozen output for the same input — any difference fails the test
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: regression test = "did anything change?" Frozen input + frozen expected output. Run the new code, compare. Catches subtle changes (a value flipped, a row reordered) that other tests might miss.

questions.question: NordGrid runs tests in a "test notebook" BEFORE the transformation notebook. The pattern is:
options: A pipeline gate — if tests fail, the ForEach never runs; bad code is blocked from production
A documentation pattern; test notebooks describe the transformation but don't execute
A capacity allocation pattern; the test notebook reserves CUs for the transformation
A user notification pattern; tests publish their results to Slack via the test notebook
questions.correctAnswer: A pipeline gate — if tests fail, the ForEach never runs; bad code is blocked from production
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: gate before the doors. Tests run first. Pass = continue. Fail = stop, alert, do nothing destructive. 15-second test notebook prevents 3-hour broken pipeline runs.

questions.question: NordGrid's 15-second test suite has prevented 12 production incidents in 6 months. The lesson is:
options: Testing investment pays back — small upfront cost prevents downstream incidents that cost much more
Tests should target 15 seconds total runtime; longer suites are dropped for being slow
Tests are mandatory for compliance; the 12 incidents would have triggered audits
Tests replace post-mortems; the 12 incidents went undocumented because tests caught them
questions.correctAnswer: Testing investment pays back — small upfront cost prevents downstream incidents that cost much more
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: ROI math is clear. 12 prevented incidents = 12 production fires that didn't happen. The 15-second test cost is trivial compared to incident response, late nights, and consumer trust loss.

questions.question: NordGrid tracked 8 deployment incidents in 6 months. Six were caused by:
options: DIRECT PRODUCTION EDITS — engineers editing the production notebook bypass review and Git history
Spark version regressions — Microsoft updates broke production code without warning
Cluster capacity issues — production deployments hit resource limits during peak hours
Network timeouts — Azure region failovers disconnected the workspace from OneLake
questions.correctAnswer: DIRECT PRODUCTION EDITS — engineers editing the production notebook bypass review and Git history
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: shortcuts kill. "Just a quick fix in production" = no review, no Git, no rollback path, no test run. CI/CD prevents direct edits and forces every change through a reviewable workflow.

questions.question: NordGrid uses three long-lived branches: develop, staging, main. The pattern is:
options: Feature → develop (PR review) → staging (auto-promote) → main (production) — progressive validation
develop and staging are throwaway; main is the only persistent branch in the repo
develop is for engineers, staging is for the analyst team, main is for the executive review
Spark requires three branches per workspace; fewer branches cause Spark to refuse deploys
questions.correctAnswer: Feature → develop (PR review) → staging (auto-promote) → main (production) — progressive validation
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: each branch represents a stage of validation. Feature branches are work-in-progress. develop = peer-reviewed. staging = integration-tested. main = production. Code progresses through all three.

questions.question: A Fabric DEPLOYMENT PIPELINE (built-in feature) does what?
options: Promotes workspace items across stages (dev → staging → prod) with deployment rules per stage
Replaces Git for version control; Fabric's deployment pipeline tracks changes natively
Schedules Data Factory pipelines; "deployment pipeline" is just a name for production schedulers
Compiles notebooks to optimized Python bytecode; deployment is the build phase of the workflow
questions.correctAnswer: Promotes workspace items across stages (dev → staging → prod) with deployment rules per stage
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Fabric's built-in promotion. Take items from dev workspace, apply rule rewrites (different Lakehouse references per stage), deploy to staging then production. Complements Git, doesn't replace it.

questions.question: "Environment-as-Code" means Fabric environments (Python libraries, Spark config) are:
options: Defined in version-controlled requirements files; deployment scripts publish them to target workspaces
Stored in OneLake; environment configurations live in a special hidden Lakehouse per workspace
Encrypted by Microsoft's key vault; engineers cannot read the config but can apply it
Generated at runtime by Catalyst; environment-as-code generates configs based on current workload
questions.correctAnswer: Defined in version-controlled requirements files; deployment scripts publish them to target workspaces
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: config in Git. requirements.txt, spark settings = files in the repo. Reviewable, diffable, testable. Deployment script applies the config to the target environment.

questions.question: NordGrid pins library versions ("pyyaml==6.0.1") instead of using "latest." Why?
options: Pinning ensures reproducibility — every environment installs the SAME version, eliminating "works on my machine"
Pinning is required by Fabric — unpinned versions cause environment publishing to fail
Pinning is faster — package managers skip dependency resolution when versions are exact
Pinning is mandatory for SOX compliance — version drift is a regulatory violation
questions.correctAnswer: Pinning ensures reproducibility — every environment installs the SAME version, eliminating "works on my machine"
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: pinned = deterministic. Different teammates install the same version. Same in dev, staging, prod. "latest" silently changes over time and breaks production unpredictably.

questions.question: NordGrid's rollback playbook has THREE mechanisms. They're ranked by speed:
options: Git revert (2 min) → deployment pipeline revert (5 min) → time travel for data (10 min)
They're all the same speed; ranking is by which platform engineer is on call when needed
Time travel first (fastest) → deployment pipeline (medium) → Git revert (slowest)
Order is irrelevant — only one mechanism is used per incident depending on the failure mode
questions.correctAnswer: Git revert (2 min) → deployment pipeline revert (5 min) → time travel for data (10 min)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: code rollback (Git) is fastest. Item rollback (deployment pipeline) handles items not in Git. Data rollback (time travel) handles when the bad code already wrote bad data.

questions.question: "Configuration drift is invisible until it causes an incident." The fix is:
options: Version-control the configuration; drift = "actual config doesn't match Git" — automatically detectable
Audit logs — drift is detected by reviewing every change manually each week
Performance monitoring — drift produces slow queries that the monitoring catches eventually
User reports — drift is detected by user complaints when their saved queries fail
questions.correctAnswer: Version-control the configuration; drift = "actual config doesn't match Git" — automatically detectable
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: Git as truth. The repo is the canonical config. Diff command shows drift. Without Git, drift is silent until it breaks something — usually too late.

questions.question: NordGrid's deployment automation has manual approval gates — but only at one stage. Where?
options: PRODUCTION promotion — automation handles dev/staging; production requires deliberate human review
EVERY stage — every promotion requires manual approval to prevent any automated mistakes
NEVER — full automation including production; faster delivery is more important than safety
STAGING — staging is the gate; production deployment is fully automated after staging tests pass
questions.correctAnswer: PRODUCTION promotion — automation handles dev/staging; production requires deliberate human review
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: automate the mechanical, gate the consequential. Tests run automatically. Promoting to staging is automatic. Production is consequential — requires human "yes, deploy this now."

questions.question: A schema change requires updating notebook + pipeline + stored procedure + SQL views + semantic model SIMULTANEOUSLY. Why?
options: Partial deployment leaves the platform inconsistent — pipeline runs while components don't match yet
Fabric only allows one item to be deployed at a time; sequential deployment is mandatory
Each item type has different deployment rules; matching them is impossible without sequential deployment
Spark requires the schema to be coordinated across items; partial deployment causes Catalyst errors
questions.correctAnswer: Partial deployment leaves the platform inconsistent — pipeline runs while components don't match yet
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: coordinated change. Notebook updated but stored procedure not? Pipeline runs, writes new schema, stored procedure crashes on missing columns. All-at-once eliminates the inconsistent window.

questions.question: NordGrid deploys to production 3 times per WEEK. The discipline that makes this safe is:
options: CI/CD — automated tests, staging validation, manual approval, fast rollback if needed
Spending 80% of engineering time on testing and only 20% on actual development work
Operating only during business hours — production deploys are forbidden outside Mon-Fri 9-5
Hiring senior engineers exclusively — only 10+ year veterans are allowed to deploy to production
questions.correctAnswer: CI/CD — automated tests, staging validation, manual approval, fast rollback if needed
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: speed + safety. Without CI/CD, frequent deploys break things often. With CI/CD, frequent deploys are routine because each one passes the same gauntlet of tests, gates, and rollback readiness.

questions.question: In Fabric's capacity model, "throttling" is:
options: A DESIGN, not a bug — when total demand exceeds capacity, lower-priority workloads slow until capacity frees up
A FAILURE — Fabric refuses queries when CU consumption exceeds the SKU limit
OPTIONAL — workspace settings control whether throttling is enabled per workspace
TIME-BASED — throttling only happens during the first 10 minutes after capacity provisioning
questions.correctAnswer: A DESIGN, not a bug — when total demand exceeds capacity, lower-priority workloads slow until capacity frees up
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: shared capacity is the proposition. Throttling is the mechanism that prevents one workload from starving others. Predictable, fair, but means heavy users compete for capacity.

questions.question: NordGrid's capacity METRICS APP is checked when:
options: After any SLA breach — if the CU timeline shows throttling, the slowdown was capacity-driven not code-driven
Daily — Microsoft requires the platform team to review capacity metrics every business morning
Weekly — capacity metrics are aggregated weekly and don't need daily review for trends
Never — the metrics app is for billing only; engineers diagnose capacity through other means
questions.correctAnswer: After any SLA breach — if the CU timeline shows throttling, the slowdown was capacity-driven not code-driven
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: triage. Slow pipeline could be code (optimize the query) or capacity (throttling). Metrics app shows which. Capacity throttling = scheduling problem. Code = optimization problem. Different fixes.

questions.question: NordGrid's "70% Rule" for scheduling means:
options: Sum of all concurrent workloads' expected CUs should not exceed 70% of capacity — 30% headroom for variability
Capacity utilization above 70% triggers automatic SKU upgrade to the next tier without admin action
Per-team CU usage capped at 70% — no team can consume more than this share without approval
Performance optimization targets — pipelines should achieve 70% of theoretical max performance
questions.correctAnswer: Sum of all concurrent workloads' expected CUs should not exceed 70% of capacity — 30% headroom for variability
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: 30% headroom absorbs spikes. If everything goes 30% slower one day (SLA still met), no throttling. Without headroom, any variance triggers throttling and cascading SLA misses.

questions.question: NordGrid bought a SECOND capacity (F32) for analytics, keeping engineering on F64. Why?
options: CAPACITY SEPARATION — no analyst can throttle the engineering pipelines; isolation by independent capacities
F64 was being deprecated; the F32 was a forced upgrade by Microsoft's policy
F32 is cheaper than F64 per-CU; running analytics on F32 saves money on aggregate workloads
F32 has different security models; analyst workloads required the F32-specific security tier
questions.correctAnswer: CAPACITY SEPARATION — no analyst can throttle the engineering pipelines; isolation by independent capacities
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: isolation when needed. Engineering needs guaranteed compute at 02:00 UTC. Analyst Power BI refresh at the same time? Fights for capacity. Two capacities = no fight, predictable performance for both.

questions.question: NordGrid's biggest CU optimization came from switching pipeline mode FROM ___ TO ___:
options: Full overwrite (450 CU-min) → MERGE (120 CU-min) — 73% reduction by avoiding unnecessary work
Manual triggers → scheduled — automation eliminated capacity spent on retry loops
Single-cluster → multi-cluster — parallelism reduced overall duration significantly
Local mode → cluster mode — proper distribution made queries 10× faster
questions.correctAnswer: Full overwrite (450 CU-min) → MERGE (120 CU-min) — 73% reduction by avoiding unnecessary work
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: incremental beats full. Overwrite re-processes everything; MERGE only the changes. At 1.3B rows, 99% of the table is unchanged — MERGE skips that work entirely.

questions.question: Analyst SQL queries are individually small but COLLECTIVELY expensive — 200 queries × 2 CU-min = 400 CU-min/day. The fix?
options: EDUCATE analysts — better query patterns (aggregations, partition filters, column selection) reduce consumption 40%
RESTRICT analysts — limit each user to 10 queries per day to cap total consumption
CACHE everything — pre-compute every possible query and serve from cache only
OFFLOAD to Spark — analyst queries run on a dedicated Spark cluster outside the Warehouse
questions.correctAnswer: EDUCATE analysts — better query patterns (aggregations, partition filters, column selection) reduce consumption 40%
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: education > restriction. Analysts adjust their query patterns when they see the cost. Restriction breeds resentment and workarounds. Awareness changes behavior.

questions.question: NordGrid uses SHOWBACK (cost reports) instead of CHARGEBACK (billing teams directly). Why?
options: Showback creates awareness without budget conflicts — visibility changes behavior without incentivizing teams to hoard
Showback is required by Fabric — chargeback is not yet available in current Fabric capacity SKUs
Showback is faster to implement — chargeback requires legal review and accounting integration
Showback is mandatory for SOX — chargeback is forbidden as a financial control practice
questions.correctAnswer: Showback creates awareness without budget conflicts — visibility changes behavior without incentivizing teams to hoard
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: governance philosophy. Chargeback creates "this is OUR budget" mentality (defensive, hoarding). Showback creates awareness (collaborative, optimization). Cultural choice.

questions.question: NordGrid scales BEFORE forced to. The reasoning is:
options: Reactive scaling takes days/weeks; proactive scaling is planned during a maintenance window with no impact
Reactive scaling is cheaper; proactive scaling pays for excess capacity that's never used
Reactive scaling is required by Fabric — you cannot purchase capacity until you hit the throttling limit
Reactive scaling is the industry default; proactive is a NordGrid-specific anomaly
questions.correctAnswer: Reactive scaling takes days/weeks; proactive scaling is planned during a maintenance window with no impact
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: quarterly capacity planning catches issues before they hit. Demand will exceed 70% in 6 months? Scale at month 4 with no user impact. Reactive = scrambling under fire.

questions.question: BURST capacity in Fabric is described as "free headroom but FINITE." Meaning:
options: Underutilized periods accumulate burst credit; heavy periods withdraw it — but burst depletes if overused
Burst is purchased separately; "free" applies only to the first 100 hours per month
Burst is unlimited; "finite" refers only to the 99-second response time limit
Burst is for emergencies; routine consumption above the SKU is forbidden contractually
questions.correctAnswer: Underutilized periods accumulate burst credit; heavy periods withdraw it — but burst depletes if overused
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: burst = banked capacity. Off-peak hours fill the bank. Heavy workloads draw from it. Free as long as you don't overdraw on average. Schedule heavy work during off-peak to maximize burst.

questions.question: Capacity governance is described as "FINANCIAL governance" because:
options: Every CU consumed is money spent; tracking + optimization = financial discipline
It is owned by the finance team, not engineering — financial governance is the formal organizational structure
It uses financial software (SAP, Oracle Financials) for reporting capacity metrics
It is exempt from technical reviews — only financial reviews govern capacity decisions
questions.correctAnswer: Every CU consumed is money spent; tracking + optimization = financial discipline
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: capacity = $$$. An unoptimized notebook = wasted CU = wasted money. Tracking, attribution, and optimization mirror financial controls.

questions.question: Without governance at NordGrid's 3-team scale, the FIRST problem to emerge is typically:
options: WORKSPACE PROLIFERATION — teams create workspaces ad-hoc, leading to duplicates and shadow data
CAPACITY EXHAUSTION — teams over-consume the shared pool without coordination
SECURITY BREACHES — teams accidentally expose sensitive data to unauthorized users
PERFORMANCE DEGRADATION — teams' queries interfere with each other, slowing everyone
questions.correctAnswer: WORKSPACE PROLIFERATION — teams create workspaces ad-hoc, leading to duplicates and shadow data
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: ungoverned scale = mess. Each team thinks "we need a quick workspace" → 30 workspaces in 6 months. Duplicates, abandoned items, no clear ownership. Governance prevents this.

questions.question: NordGrid's "hybrid" workspace pattern combines:
options: Layer-based for shared production (Bronze/Silver/Gold) AND team-based for team-private experimentation
Region-based and time-based — workspaces split by geographic region and shift schedules
Public and private — Microsoft's standard pattern with one public workspace and one private per team
Read and write — separate workspaces for reading existing data vs writing new data
questions.correctAnswer: Layer-based for shared production (Bronze/Silver/Gold) AND team-based for team-private experimentation
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: hybrid = different patterns for different needs. Production = layer-based (shared, owned by engineering). Team experimentation = team-based (each team has their own sandbox).

questions.question: Fabric's 3 security layers are workspace roles, OneLake data access roles, and:
options: Row/column-level security in the Warehouse — fine-grained per-row and per-column access
Microsoft Purview labels — sensitivity classification is the third security mechanism in Fabric
Network firewalls — IP allowlists are the third layer at the network level
Encryption keys — per-table key management is the third layer for data-at-rest protection
questions.correctAnswer: Row/column-level security in the Warehouse — fine-grained per-row and per-column access
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: defense in depth. Workspace = coarse (whole workspace). OneLake roles = medium (which tables). Warehouse RLS/CLS = fine (which rows, which columns). Each layer adds protection.

questions.question: NordGrid's ML team needs meter consumption patterns but NOT customer_type. The solution is:
options: COLUMN MASKING — customer_type column is masked for ML team users via SQL endpoint policy
A SEPARATE WORKSPACE — copy meter data without the customer_type column for ML team only
A NIGHTLY EXPORT — generate a daily file without customer_type for the ML team to consume
API GATEWAY — block customer_type field at the network layer for ML team identities
questions.correctAnswer: COLUMN MASKING — customer_type column is masked for ML team users via SQL endpoint policy
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: column masking = "you can see this row, but not this column." Specific to user role. Avoids data duplication; one table, different views per consumer.

questions.question: Microsoft Purview SENSITIVITY LABELS classify Fabric items. The labels TRIGGER:
options: PROTECTION POLICIES — Confidential blocks export to personal email; Highly Confidential blocks unmanaged devices
COMPRESSION RULES — labeled data is compressed differently per sensitivity level for storage
PERFORMANCE PRIORITIES — Confidential items get faster query execution; General items deprioritized
BACKUP FREQUENCIES — labels determine how often Microsoft backs up the labeled item
questions.correctAnswer: PROTECTION POLICIES — Confidential blocks export to personal email; Highly Confidential blocks unmanaged devices
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: labels aren't metadata — they're enforced. "Confidential" attached to a Lakehouse → users can't download to personal devices. Real DLP enforcement, not just classification.

questions.question: Fabric DOMAINS at NordGrid scale are used to:
options: DELEGATE governance — domain admins handle their domain's policies; platform team isn't a bottleneck
CONSOLIDATE billing — domain costs aggregate for departmental chargeback
PHYSICALLY ISOLATE data — different domains live in different Azure regions for residency
ENFORCE schemas — domain-level schemas override workspace schemas automatically
questions.correctAnswer: DELEGATE governance — domain admins handle their domain's policies; platform team isn't a bottleneck
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: scale governance through delegation. Without domains, every governance decision flows through the platform team. With domains, domain admins handle their area. Platform team scales.

questions.question: After NordGrid's engineering team got CERTIFIED status on the Silver Lakehouse, what happened?
options: The analytics team retired their shadow tables — Certified communicated trust; they migrated to Silver
The capacity bill increased by 20% — Certified items have a premium pricing tier
Microsoft Purview revoked Confidential labels — Certified items are public by default
Power BI auto-generated reports for the Lakehouse — Certified items get auto-reporting feature
questions.correctAnswer: The analytics team retired their shadow tables — Certified communicated trust; they migrated to Silver
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: certification = trust signal. Without it, teams build their own copies "just in case." With Certified status visible in the catalog, teams adopt the official source. Reduces sprawl.

questions.question: NordGrid's regulator requires "annual proof that customer data is accessed only by authorized personnel." The mechanism is:
options: AUDIT LOGS — every access event recorded; queryable to prove compliance with access policies
ANNUAL SURVEY — engineers self-certify that access is appropriate for their role
MICROSOFT CERTIFICATION — paying for an external auditor to attest to platform compliance
PURGE & RECREATE — annually wipe the platform and rebuild access from scratch
questions.correctAnswer: AUDIT LOGS — every access event recorded; queryable to prove compliance with access policies
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: audit logs = proof. Logged who accessed which sensitive item, when, for how long. Regulator queries the logs annually. Without audit logs, "trust us" doesn't satisfy regulators.
