questions.question: NordGrid's rule: "Write the runbook when you build the pipeline, not when it breaks." Why?
options: During development, the engineer remembers WHY decisions were made; afterward, that context is lost
Runbook authors must be senior engineers; junior engineers can't write valid runbooks anyway
Runbooks must be approved before pipeline deployment; they're a compliance gate
Runbooks have to be Markdown; the formatting takes longer if written under incident pressure
questions.correctAnswer: During development, the engineer remembers WHY decisions were made; afterward, that context is lost
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: capture context while it's fresh. "Why retry 3 times?" "Why this timeout?" Built-time runbooks have rationale. Incident-time runbooks are panicked stubs.

questions.question: Governance is described as "the platform's IMMUNE SYSTEM." Meaning:
options: Without governance, every new team/workload/dataset introduces risk; governance contains and prevents that risk
Governance literally inspects code for malware; it is a security scanner for the platform
Governance audits financial transactions; only financial governance affects compliance
Governance is bureaucratic overhead; it slows innovation but is required by IT departments
questions.correctAnswer: Without governance, every new team/workload/dataset introduces risk; governance contains and prevents that risk
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: immune system analogy. Each new addition (new team, dataset, workload) is a potential threat. Governance (roles, labels, audits, runbooks) ensures the platform stays healthy as it grows.

questions.question: Building a pipeline is a PROJECT. Operating a platform is a:
options: PRACTICE — without an end; the pipeline runs every night and operations continue indefinitely
PROJECT — operations have defined milestones and end dates per business cycle
SCRIPT — pre-defined sequence of operational activities executed daily
CONTRACT — formal agreement with consumers that defines operational obligations
questions.correctAnswer: PRACTICE — without an end; the pipeline runs every night and operations continue indefinitely
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: mindset shift. Project = build, deliver, done. Practice = operate continuously, never finished. Operations is daily craft.

questions.question: "Error budgets" make abstract reliability concrete. Example: 99% SLO means:
options: You can fail 3.65 times per year; the budget says explicitly that some failures are acceptable
You must succeed 99% on every individual run; one failure invalidates the SLA permanently
You can fail unlimited times if recovery is fast — only failure DURATION counts toward the SLA
The 1% applies to data accuracy; pipelines must always succeed but data can be 1% wrong
questions.correctAnswer: You can fail 3.65 times per year; the budget says explicitly that some failures are acceptable
questions.explanation: Lesson 1 (closing): error budgets convert abstract "high reliability" into specific allowance. 99% = 1% failures = 3.65/year. If under budget = ship features. Over = halt and improve reliability.

questions.question: NordGrid's "morning check" dashboard is designed for a 2-MINUTE review. The principle is:
options: Five binary indicators (green/red) sourced from existing metrics; 2 minutes catches 95% of overnight issues
A detailed report — the 2 minutes is for reading the report after it generates
A SQL query — the 2 minutes is for the query to execute against the metrics tables
A 30-page document — the 2 minutes is the executive summary at the top
questions.correctAnswer: Five binary indicators (green/red) sourced from existing metrics; 2 minutes catches 95% of overnight issues
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: design dashboards for the decision they support. Morning = "is something wrong?" Binary indicators answer fastest. Trend analysis (different decision) gets a different dashboard.

questions.question: Every NordGrid incident gets a POST-MORTEM, even minor ones. Why?
options: Post-mortems convert incidents into platform improvements; without them, the same issue recurs
Compliance — auditors require formal documentation of every event that affects production
Performance metrics — post-mortems are the data source for SLA reports each quarter
Onboarding — new engineers read post-mortems to learn the platform's history
questions.correctAnswer: Post-mortems convert incidents into platform improvements; without them, the same issue recurs
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: even minor incidents reveal weaknesses. Schema validation now catches issues early because of one minor incident's post-mortem. Without writing it down, the lesson is lost.

questions.question: A good RUNBOOK passes "the runbook test." What's the test?
options: A new team member can follow it at 03:00 AM without asking questions — every step is unambiguous
Senior engineers review and approve it annually; runbooks must have signed-off versions
It loads in under 100ms — runbooks are designed for fast incident response display
It contains exactly 10 steps; longer runbooks are split, shorter combined
questions.correctAnswer: A new team member can follow it at 03:00 AM without asking questions — every step is unambiguous
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 03:00 AM = no senior backup, no calling in. Either the runbook is precise enough to act on alone, or it's useless. Test it during drills with newest team member.

questions.question: NordGrid's data freshness SLA is set at "pipeline expected completion + buffer," NOT at consumer usage time. Why?
options: Detects breaches early — alerts fire when the pipeline misses, not when consumers complain
Uses production data — the SLA is automatically aligned with actual completion times observed
Required by Microsoft — SLAs must reference Fabric metrics, not consumer behavior
Saves capacity — alerts at consumer time would query the table repeatedly, wasting CUs
questions.correctAnswer: Detects breaches early — alerts fire when the pipeline misses, not when consumers complain
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: alert before pain. Pipeline expected at 02:30. Consumer arrives at 08:00. SLA at 03:00 catches issues at 02:30 with hours to fix. SLA at 08:00 catches issues when consumers are already affected.

questions.question: "Slow decline is harder to detect than sudden failure." Why?
options: A pipeline at 14 min today, 14.3 next month, doesn't trigger any alert — but trend exceeds SLA in 18 months
Sudden failures produce stack traces; slow decline produces no logs at all
Slow decline is invisible to users; sudden failure is reported immediately
Sudden failures are caught by Spark; slow decline requires custom monitoring code
questions.correctAnswer: A pipeline at 14 min today, 14.3 next month, doesn't trigger any alert — but trend exceeds SLA in 18 months
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: cliff vs slope. Cliff (failure) = obvious. Slope (gradual degradation) = invisible until you cross the SLA threshold. Trend monitoring catches what point-in-time alerts miss.

questions.question: NordGrid follows the "STRANGLER FIG" pattern for handling deprecations. The pattern is:
options: Build new alongside old, validate they produce identical results, switch consumers, decommission old after monitoring
Replace old with new immediately; "strangler fig" refers to the speed of replacement
Keep old indefinitely; "strangler fig" refers to letting it remain alongside new without action
Migrate consumers in random order; "strangler fig" refers to organic adoption patterns
questions.correctAnswer: Build new alongside old, validate they produce identical results, switch consumers, decommission old after monitoring
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: parallel migration. Both run. Compare results. Cut over when confident. Monitor. Decommission old. Each step is reversible if issues appear.

questions.question: NordGrid's 3-question framework for evaluating new Fabric features is:
options: (1) Does it solve a current problem? (2) What is the adoption cost? (3) What is the risk?
(1) Is it from Microsoft? (2) Is it free? (3) Will competitors adopt it?
(1) Is it stable? (2) Is it fast? (3) Is it documented?
(1) Will analysts like it? (2) Will engineers like it? (3) Will managers like it?
questions.correctAnswer: (1) Does it solve a current problem? (2) What is the adoption cost? (3) What is the risk?
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: feature evaluation framework. Microsoft releases dozens of features. Adopt only those that solve actual problems with acceptable cost and risk. Skip the noise.

questions.question: The mid-level → senior transition is described as "thinking differently about what 'done' means." At mid-level, "done" is:
options: OPERATIONAL — pipeline runs reliably, monitored, governed, with documented runbooks
CODED — the pipeline runs successfully one time on real data
DEPLOYED — the code is in the production workspace and configured properly
TESTED — the pipeline passes unit and integration tests
questions.correctAnswer: OPERATIONAL — pipeline runs reliably, monitored, governed, with documented runbooks
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: definitions of done. Junior = "code works." Mid = "operates reliably." Senior = "scales across teams and time." Each level adds dimensions: reliability, governance, observability, evolution.

questions.question: The senior-level shift from mid-level is described as:
options: From designing pipelines to designing the platform other engineers build pipelines on
From building one pipeline to building 100 pipelines using the same patterns
From PySpark to T-SQL — senior work happens entirely in the Warehouse, not in notebooks
From single-cloud to multi-cloud — senior platforms span Azure, AWS, and GCP simultaneously
questions.correctAnswer: From designing pipelines to designing the platform other engineers build pipelines on
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: senior = platform engineering. Catalog hierarchies, ownership contracts, observability, governance — work that enables OTHER engineers to deliver. Different "done" definition than mid-level.

questions.question: GridUnion uses a 4-segment workspace namespace: `ws_{country}_{layer}_{environment}`. Why country FIRST?
options: Most-frequent operation is country-level work; country-first makes sortable lists group meaningfully
Required by EU regulations — country must precede other segments in any data engineering namespace
Spark requires alphabetical sorting on country; other orders break the workspace listing
Country abbreviations are 2 chars; placing them first keeps the namespace short by convention
questions.correctAnswer: Most-frequent operation is country-level work; country-first makes sortable lists group meaningfully
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: optimize for daily ops. German engineers see all `ws_de_*` together. Sortable, browseable, searchable. Layer-first or env-first scatters country resources across the list.

questions.question: A French workspace creates a SHORTCUT to a German Lakehouse table. The security model is:
options: The shortcut INHERITS the German Lakehouse's data access roles — French team needs German-side permissions
The shortcut ADOPTS the French workspace's permissions — French team controls who can read it
The shortcut runs unauthenticated — Fabric assumes shortcuts to internal items don't need authorization
The shortcut requires a DUPLICATE permission setup — both German and French sides must grant access
questions.correctAnswer: The shortcut INHERITS the German Lakehouse's data access roles — French team needs German-side permissions
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: shortcuts inherit source security. The French team is governed by German team's access policies. This is asymmetric — different from copies, where the destination team controls access.

questions.question: A "data product" differs from a "table" by:
options: Having declared schema, freshness SLA, quality contract, owner team, and consumer registration in a manifest
Being stored in the Platinum layer; tables live in lower layers without product status
Being read-only — data products cannot be modified after creation
Being certified by Microsoft — only Microsoft-certified items qualify as data products
questions.correctAnswer: Having declared schema, freshness SLA, quality contract, owner team, and consumer registration in a manifest
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: at 200+ tables and 80 engineers, personal trust doesn't scale. Manifests turn data into products with explicit guarantees.

questions.question: GridUnion enforces "single ownership" per item. Without enforcement, the registry is:
options: A SUGGESTION, not a contract — engineers from other teams can write to any Lakehouse, breaking ownership
A read-only document that always reflects the current state automatically
A backup mechanism for restoring deleted items based on their registered owner
An audit trail required by GDPR; without it, the platform fails compliance checks
questions.correctAnswer: A SUGGESTION, not a contract — engineers from other teams can write to any Lakehouse, breaking ownership
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: declaring ownership is necessary but not sufficient. Workspace roles must enforce write restrictions to the owner team. Otherwise the registry is documentation that can be ignored.

questions.question: GridUnion adds a 4th PLATINUM layer above Gold. The justification requires:
options: Cross-unit data is a frequent query pattern (not occasional) AND multiple consumers need it
A new Microsoft license tier — Platinum is a paid feature requiring annual subscription
A separate Spark cluster — Platinum requires dedicated infrastructure for compliance reasons
Approval from the EU regulators — Platinum data products must pre-approve before creation
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-unit data is a frequent query pattern (not occasional) AND multiple consumers need it
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: Platinum is for cross-domain joins that get queried repeatedly. Once is materialization; many consumers is architectural reuse worth the layer.

questions.question: GridUnion uses publish-subscribe for cross-team pipeline dependencies. The benefit over direct invocation?
options: Decouples teams — producer doesn't need to know about consumers; consumers subscribe to events independently
Faster execution — pub-sub is 10× faster than direct pipeline invocation in Fabric
Required by Fabric — direct invocation across teams is forbidden by platform policy
Simpler error handling — pub-sub auto-handles all failures without explicit retry logic
questions.correctAnswer: Decouples teams — producer doesn't need to know about consumers; consumers subscribe to events independently
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: tight coupling = cascading delays. Pub-sub = "I published, anyone can consume." Producer team doesn't need to know who waits on them.

questions.question: GridUnion's 140 config files are 80% INHERITED from 2 global files. The pattern is:
options: Hierarchical config — global → country → environment → workspace, each layer overrides the layer above
Random distribution — config files are placed wherever convenient at creation time
Replicated config — every workspace has its own copy of all 140 files for performance
Encrypted overlays — country files encrypt sections of the global files for privacy
questions.correctAnswer: Hierarchical config — global → country → environment → workspace, each layer overrides the layer above
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: layered config = sane defaults. Most settings inherit. Only true overrides exist as explicit files. Reduces duplication and makes the platform-wide change a one-file edit.

questions.question: GridUnion's morning check at platform scale uses AGGREGATE SLIs (e.g. "% pipelines on time"). Why not check each pipeline individually?
options: At 500+ pipelines, individual check is impractical — aggregates surface where to drill down
Aggregate SLIs are required by Fabric — individual checks were deprecated in 2024
Aggregate SLIs are 100× faster — individual checks would consume too much capacity
Aggregate SLIs predict the future — individual checks only show current state
questions.correctAnswer: At 500+ pipelines, individual check is impractical — aggregates surface where to drill down
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: scale changes the question. NordGrid (5 pipelines): check each. GridUnion (500+): "are 99% on time?" → drill down to the 1%.

questions.question: GridUnion's 12 teams produce 45 data products. The platform team's role is:
options: Build the platform that ENABLES other teams' products — not build products themselves
Build all 45 data products centrally — the 12 teams consume what the platform team produces
Approve every data product before creation — the platform team is the bottleneck for new products
Replace the 12 teams over time — central platform team will eventually own all products
questions.correctAnswer: Build the platform that ENABLES other teams' products — not build products themselves
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: platform = enabler. 12 teams produce 45 products on top of platform infrastructure (catalog, contracts, observability). Without platform team, each team rebuilds the same plumbing.

questions.question: Catalyst rules are organized into BATCHES that execute in a strict ORDER. Why?
options: Earlier batches' optimizations enable later batches — pruning enables filter pushdown enables join reorder
Spark requires ordered execution; randomized order produces incorrect plans in some cases
Each batch is owned by a different team within Microsoft — order reflects organizational priorities
Order determines billing — earlier batches cost less than later ones in capacity calculations
questions.correctAnswer: Earlier batches' optimizations enable later batches — pruning enables filter pushdown enables join reorder
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: rule dependencies. Pruning unused columns first means later filter pushdown has fewer columns to consider. Order = correctness + completeness of optimization.

questions.question: A 5-join pipeline with 10× selectivity error per filter compounds to:
options: 100,000× — error compounds MULTIPLICATIVELY through plan depth (10^5 = 100,000)
50× — error scales linearly with number of filters (5 × 10 = 50)
10× — Catalyst clamps cumulative error to the maximum single-filter error
0× — AQE corrects all upstream errors at runtime, leaving zero residual error
questions.correctAnswer: 100,000× — error compounds MULTIPLICATIVELY through plan depth (10^5 = 100,000)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: estimation errors compound. Each filter's error multiplies. Deep plans become wildly off, leading to bad join strategies. Materialization boundaries break the chain.

questions.question: Manual hints (e.g. `BROADCAST`) are described as "the escape hatch when the cost model fails." The policy is:
options: Every hint must include a comment explaining WHY — without it, future engineers can't evaluate whether the hint still applies
Hints are forbidden in production — only the cost model can decide join strategies
Every hint must be approved by Microsoft — Catalyst hints are platform-controlled features
Hints expire after 30 days — Spark requires periodic re-validation of all manual overrides
questions.correctAnswer: Every hint must include a comment explaining WHY — without it, future engineers can't evaluate whether the hint still applies
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: hints are knowledge the cost model lacks. The "why" comment captures that knowledge so it survives refactors. Without it, the hint becomes magic that nobody dares touch.

questions.question: AQE's value over CBO is that AQE uses:
options: ACTUAL runtime stats (post-shuffle data sizes) instead of compile-time estimates which may be stale
Different optimization rules — AQE has rules CBO doesn't, which is why it's preferred
A faster cost model — AQE's formulas execute 10× faster than CBO's formulas
Manual hints as input — AQE reads hints; CBO ignores them entirely
questions.correctAnswer: ACTUAL runtime stats (post-shuffle data sizes) instead of compile-time estimates which may be stale
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: CBO guesses. AQE measures. Once a shuffle completes, AQE knows actual partition sizes — it can re-decide partition count, broadcast conversion, or skew splitting based on real data.

questions.question: V-ORDER is a Fabric-PROPRIETARY optimization. Why might GridUnion still adopt it for production?
options: Reads on V-Ordered tables are 30-50% faster — Microsoft's engine has optimized read paths for V-Ordered Parquet
V-Order is required for SOX compliance — non-V-Ordered tables fail audit requirements
V-Order doubles storage capacity — the proprietary format compresses 2× better than standard
V-Order is the only way to use OneLake — non-V-Ordered files cannot be stored in OneLake at all
questions.correctAnswer: Reads on V-Ordered tables are 30-50% faster — Microsoft's engine has optimized read paths for V-Ordered Parquet
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: vendor lock-in vs performance trade-off. V-Order is Fabric-only, but the read speedup is meaningful. Acceptable when you're committed to Fabric.

questions.question: A filter on a DERIVED column (e.g. `consumption_tier`) often blocks pushdown. The fix:
options: Push the filter to the SOURCE column instead (e.g. `daily_kw > 500` instead of `consumption_tier = "high"`)
Cast the derived column — Catalyst can push filters on cast results but not on raw derived columns
Add the derived column to the partition scheme — pushdown works on any partition column regardless of derivation
Disable pushdown for that query — derived columns work better without pushdown attempts
questions.correctAnswer: Push the filter to the SOURCE column instead (e.g. `daily_kw > 500` instead of `consumption_tier = "high"`)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: derived columns aren't in Parquet metadata. Filter on the underlying column that IS in metadata, then derive. Pushes the filter to the storage layer.

questions.question: Catalyst's join strategy DECISION TREE has a priority chain. Highest priority is:
options: Broadcast hash join if one side is below `autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` — cheapest, no large-side shuffle
Sort-merge join — Catalyst's default to ensure consistent behavior across data sizes
Shuffled hash join — Catalyst always prefers it for any join over 100MB total
Cross join — Catalyst tries cross join first to detect missing join conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Broadcast hash join if one side is below `autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` — cheapest, no large-side shuffle
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: priority = cheapest first. Broadcast eliminates shuffle on the large side. Sort-merge is the fallback for large-large joins.

questions.question: A pipeline ran stably for 3 months, then suddenly took 38 minutes instead of 14 — code unchanged. Likely cause?
options: PLAN REGRESSION — Catalyst's optimizer chose a different plan due to data growth or stats changes
Capacity throttling — the workspace was assigned a smaller SKU recently
Spark version regression — Microsoft pushed an update that slowed all queries
Network issue — the underlying Azure region had degraded performance for the day
questions.correctAnswer: PLAN REGRESSION — Catalyst's optimizer chose a different plan due to data growth or stats changes
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: silent plan changes are the most dangerous. Same code, different plan = different performance. Plan fingerprinting catches these regressions before users notice.

questions.question: Nested column pruning (Spark 3.0+) prunes individual fields WITHIN structs. The benefit at SCADA scale:
options: A query accessing one field in a 200-field struct reads ~10 bytes instead of ~2KB per row — dramatic I/O reduction
Spark requires nested pruning for struct columns; without it, struct columns cannot be queried
Nested pruning is required by Microsoft Purview to apply column-level security to nested fields
Nested pruning makes joins 10× faster — it's primarily a join optimization, not an I/O optimization
questions.correctAnswer: A query accessing one field in a 200-field struct reads ~10 bytes instead of ~2KB per row — dramatic I/O reduction
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: nested pruning at scale. SCADA telemetry has 200-field structs. Without pruning, every query reads all 200. With pruning, only the accessed fields. Order-of-magnitude I/O reduction.

questions.question: GridUnion's "quarterly optimizer review" of top 20 notebooks examines plan FINGERPRINT history. The goal:
options: Detect plan regressions early — fingerprint changes signal potential performance issues before they breach SLA
Document architecture — the fingerprints become the plan documentation for compliance
Train new engineers — fingerprints are educational artifacts for onboarding
Generate reports — fingerprint counts are aggregated into Microsoft's capacity reports
questions.correctAnswer: Detect plan regressions early — fingerprint changes signal potential performance issues before they breach SLA
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: proactive performance management. A change in fingerprint = something changed at the optimizer level. Investigate before it breaches SLA — quarterly review catches drift before it accumulates.

questions.question: GridUnion's partition design is described as "POLITICAL negotiation, not technical optimization." Why political?
options: Different teams want different partition orderings; the design must satisfy multiple competing query patterns
Partition decisions require Microsoft approval — the political process is purely external
Country-level regulators dictate partition columns — the design must follow regulatory edicts
Each team has veto power on partition changes — consensus is mandatory for any design
questions.correctAnswer: Different teams want different partition orderings; the design must satisfy multiple competing query patterns
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: German billing wants date-first. French regulator wants country-first. The "right" answer is a multi-objective compromise, not a calculation.

questions.question: GridUnion's 24-MONTH partition projection serves what purpose?
options: Predicts when current scheme will exceed limits (directory count, partition size) — repartition before crisis
Required by SOX compliance — financial data partitioning must project 24 months by regulation
Aligns with Microsoft's billing cycles — capacity planning requires 24-month projections
Determines tax accounting — partition decisions are capital expenditures requiring 24-month plans
questions.correctAnswer: Predicts when current scheme will exceed limits (directory count, partition size) — repartition before crisis
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: partition design has a SHELF LIFE. Today's scheme works; in 18 months it may break (too many directories, partitions too big). Project ahead to plan migrations during off-peak windows.

questions.question: Z-ORDER skips at the ROW-GROUP level (within files); partition pruning skips at the DIRECTORY level. The relationship:
options: They are ADDITIVE — partition pruning eliminates directories; Z-ORDER eliminates row groups within remaining files
They are ALTERNATIVES — choose one or the other; using both produces conflicting metadata
They are IDENTICAL — Z-ORDER is the new name for partition pruning since Spark 3.5
They are EXCLUSIVE — Fabric requires choosing exactly one; Spark refuses to combine them
questions.correctAnswer: They are ADDITIVE — partition pruning eliminates directories; Z-ORDER eliminates row groups within remaining files
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: complementary granularities. Pruning eliminates 90% of files (skip directories). Z-ORDER eliminates 80% of remaining row groups (skip parts of remaining files). Layered I/O reduction.

questions.question: Counterintuitive: a `BROADCAST` hint can DISABLE Dynamic Partition Pruning. Why?
options: DPP requires the small side to be processed FIRST; broadcast hints can change processing order, breaking DPP's prerequisite
Broadcast hints disable AQE; DPP requires AQE to be active during the join
Broadcast hints are mutually exclusive with DPP by Spark configuration design
Broadcast hints lock the partition scheme; DPP requires dynamic partition selection
questions.correctAnswer: DPP requires the small side to be processed FIRST; broadcast hints can change processing order, breaking DPP's prerequisite
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: counter-intuitive interaction. Broadcast hints are usually helpful but can disable DPP. Verify both DPP and broadcast in the plan after adding hints.

questions.question: At enterprise scale, "small files" become a SYSTEMIC problem because:
options: OneLake metadata operations cost real money; millions of small files inflate per-query overhead dramatically
Small files exceed Spark's file count limit of 1 million per workspace and writes will start to fail outright
Small files corrupt during compaction; only files above the size threshold survive maintenance reliably
Small files are encrypted differently than large files; mixing them violates Fabric workspace compliance policies
questions.correctAnswer: OneLake metadata operations cost real money; millions of small files inflate per-query overhead dramatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: cloud storage is metadata-billed. Each file open = a billed API call. Millions of files × millions of queries = significant cost. OPTIMIZE compaction is cost-control.

questions.question: Bucketing requires "matching bucket counts" across joined tables. The implication for changes:
options: Changing bucket count is an ARCHITECTURAL commitment — every joined partner must rebuild
Bucket count can be changed freely; Fabric auto-rebalances during the next OPTIMIZE
Bucket count is informational; changes have no effect on joins
Bucket count is per-query; each query specifies its own count at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Changing bucket count is an ARCHITECTURAL commitment — every joined partner must rebuild
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: bucketing creates cross-table dependencies. Choose 256 buckets and every table joined with it must match. Adding a 257-bucket partner = falls back to shuffle. Coordinate via bucket registry.

questions.question: Repartitioning a 15TB live Delta table with 12 dependent pipelines requires:
options: A 90-day timeline with consumer notification, validation period, dual-write, and gradual migration
A weekend maintenance window — repartition Friday night, restart Monday morning
Microsoft support ticket — repartitioning at this scale requires Microsoft Engineering involvement
Capacity SKU upgrade — F64 cannot handle 15TB rewrites; F128 is required
questions.correctAnswer: A 90-day timeline with consumer notification, validation period, dual-write, and gradual migration
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 15TB repartition isn't maintenance — it's a platform migration. Coordinated change requiring weeks of execution + months of consumer migration. The cheapest version costs huge organizational effort.

questions.question: GridUnion uses HYBRID dual-write + CRAS for repartition. Why both?
options: Dual-write for the historical archive (no downtime); CRAS for current month (atomic switchover)
Required by the Delta protocol — both are mandatory for any repartition operation
Cost optimization — dual-write is cheaper for old data, CRAS is cheaper for new
Compliance — historical data requires dual-write per SOX, current data requires CRAS per BNetzA
questions.correctAnswer: Dual-write for the historical archive (no downtime); CRAS for current month (atomic switchover)
questions.explanation: Lesson 7 closing: pragmatic combination. Dual-write archive (incremental, slow but safe) + CRAS current (fast, atomic). Best of both: no downtime + clean cutover.

questions.question: Why monitor the MAINTENANCE SYSTEM (the OPTIMIZE job itself) and not just the tables?
options: The maintenance job can fail silently — without monitoring, file counts grow until tables degrade
Maintenance runs in dedicated capacity — separate monitoring is required for billing reasons
Maintenance is run by Microsoft — internal monitoring is the only visibility for users
Maintenance schedules are unpredictable — monitoring tracks when they happen
questions.correctAnswer: The maintenance job can fail silently — without monitoring, file counts grow until tables degrade
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: meta-monitoring. The maintenance system is itself production code. If it fails, no tables get compacted, file counts grow, queries slow down — all silently. Monitor the maintainer, not just the maintained.

questions.question: GridUnion's partition GOVERNANCE is described as "the cheapest form of partition optimization." Why?
options: A 30-minute design review prevents the 6-week repartitioning project that's required to fix bad design
Governance reviews are free for Fabric customers; optimization tools cost money
Microsoft pays organizations for participating in design reviews; it's a profit center
Reviews replace the need for engineers; the platform team scales infinitely with governance
questions.correctAnswer: A 30-minute design review prevents the 6-week repartitioning project that's required to fix bad design
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: prevention >> cure. Repartitioning a live table is the most expensive data engineering task. A short design review at table creation pays for itself many times over.

questions.question: GridUnion's join optimization scope SHIFTS from individual joins to:
options: Join TOPOLOGIES — star, chain, fan-in patterns — each requires different optimization strategies
Cluster topology — physical machine layout determines join performance more than logical patterns
Network topology — Azure region selection is the primary join performance lever
Team topology — which team owns the joined tables determines optimization priorities
questions.correctAnswer: Join TOPOLOGIES — star, chain, fan-in patterns — each requires different optimization strategies
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: at scale, individual join tuning misses the bigger picture. Star = independent dimension joins. Chain = compound errors. Each topology pattern needs its own playbook.

questions.question: GridUnion's "bucket registry" coordinates bucket counts across teams. Without it:
options: Teams pick incompatible bucket counts; shuffle-free joins between their tables become impossible at scale
Bucketing performs slower because each team's bucket scheme conflicts with the others at write time
Microsoft refuses to allow bucketing without a registry — it's a strict platform-level requirement
The Fabric catalog rejects bucketed tables without a registry approval workflow being in place
questions.correctAnswer: Teams pick incompatible bucket counts; shuffle-free joins between their tables become impossible at scale
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: bucket count = cross-team contract. German team picks 256 buckets. French team picks 128. Joining their tables = full shuffle (no benefit). Registry coordinates so counts align.

questions.question: Range joins (`event_time BETWEEN start AND end`) are SLOW because:
options: Hash partitioning doesn't apply to ranges — joins fall back to nested loops without specific optimization
Spark refuses range joins entirely — they must be rewritten as equality joins
Range joins use a single thread by design — parallelism is impossible for range conditions
Range joins consume 10× more memory than equality joins regardless of data size
questions.correctAnswer: Hash partitioning doesn't apply to ranges — joins fall back to nested loops without specific optimization
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: equality joins use hash (matching keys go to same partition). Ranges have no equivalent — every row on left must check against every row on right unless specifically optimized (broadcast or bin-based).

questions.question: Catalyst eliminates a JOIN when:
options: No column from the joined table appears in SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY (only the join key for matching)
The joined table is smaller than the broadcast threshold; small tables are auto-eliminated
AQE detects zero rows in the joined table at runtime and removes the join from the plan
The joined table has no statistics; CBO conservatively eliminates joins it can't cost-estimate
questions.correctAnswer: No column from the joined table appears in SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY (only the join key for matching)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: if no columns from a join contribute to output, the join was unused. Catalyst proves uniqueness on join key, then drops the join. Wide views with selective consumers benefit hugely.

questions.question: Bloom filter pushdown vs DPP — the granularity difference is:
options: DPP skips at the partition-DIRECTORY level (coarse); Bloom filters skip at the row-group/file level (fine)
Bloom filters skip per-row; DPP skips entire stages of the plan
They are equivalent — Bloom filter is the new name for DPP starting in Spark 3.5
DPP works only on partitioned tables; Bloom filters work only on bucketed tables
questions.correctAnswer: DPP skips at the partition-DIRECTORY level (coarse); Bloom filters skip at the row-group/file level (fine)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: complementary, not redundant. DPP eliminates directories. Bloom filters eliminate row groups within remaining directories. Both can apply to the same query.

questions.question: Skew at GridUnion scale is treated as ARCHITECTURAL, not just runtime. The implication:
options: Sometimes the right fix is REDESIGNING the join key (e.g. composite key) instead of salting at every query site
Skew is solved by hardware — adding executors with more memory absorbs imbalance without code changes
Catalyst includes a built-in skew detector that auto-rewrites queries; manual intervention is rare
Skew is a Spark version regression that resolves itself with the next Spark upgrade automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Sometimes the right fix is REDESIGNING the join key (e.g. composite key) instead of salting at every query site
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: salting fixes one query. Key redesign fixes every query that joins on that key. Architectural fix scales; per-query salting doesn't.

questions.question: In an 8-hop join chain, "MATERIALIZATION BOUNDARIES" are inserted to:
options: Reset CBO statistics with FRESH row counts — prevents error compounding through plan depth
Skip remaining joins when intermediate counts are zero — short-circuits unnecessary work
Convert sort-merge to broadcast by caching intermediate results in driver memory
Distribute joins across YARN queues so different teams own different segments
questions.correctAnswer: Reset CBO statistics with FRESH row counts — prevents error compounding through plan depth
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: deep chains compound estimation errors. Materializing to Delta forces ANALYZE-style fresh stats. Downstream joins make decisions on real numbers, not 100,000×-off estimates.

questions.question: For cross-workspace joins, GridUnion prefers OneLake SHORTCUTS over Warehouse cross-database queries when:
options: Spark notebook joins benefit — shortcuts give Spark direct OneLake access without going through SQL
Always — shortcuts are universally faster than cross-database queries in every Fabric scenario
Never — cross-database queries are always preferred over shortcuts at GridUnion-style scale
When the joined data is sensitive — shortcuts have stronger encryption than cross-database queries
questions.correctAnswer: Spark notebook joins benefit — shortcuts give Spark direct OneLake access without going through SQL
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: pick the path. Spark notebook → shortcut (direct file access). T-SQL Warehouse → cross-database query. Each is the natural mechanism for its tool.

questions.question: At 15TB+ join scale, the optimization strategy SHIFTS from "avoid shuffle" to:
options: "MANAGE shuffle" — at scale shuffle is unavoidable; tune disk I/O, memory, and partition count
"Cache everything" — keep all 15TB pinned in cluster memory for the entire duration of the join
"Avoid all joins" — rewrite the pipelines to never perform joins above 10TB of input data
"Use only Warehouse" — Spark doesn't scale to 15TB joins; use T-SQL Warehouse exclusively
questions.correctAnswer: "MANAGE shuffle" — at scale shuffle is unavoidable; tune disk I/O, memory, and partition count
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: at 15TB+, even bucketing has alignment costs. Shuffle becomes inevitable. The optimization shifts to making shuffle survivable: enough disk, memory, partitions.

questions.question: The "join architecture review" is described as the highest-ROI 30-minute meeting. Why?
options: Join operations consume ~60% of total CU at GridUnion scale; one architectural improvement saves significant capacity
Reviews are required by Fabric for any pipeline; without one, Fabric refuses production deployment
Microsoft pays organizations for join architecture reviews; they're a profit center
Reviews replace all other optimization work; one review per pipeline is sufficient
questions.correctAnswer: Join operations consume ~60% of total CU at GridUnion scale; one architectural improvement saves significant capacity
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: ROI math. 60% of CUs go to joins. A 40% reduction = 24% total CU saved. Multiply by lifetime of pipeline = the 30-minute review pays back many years.

questions.question: Schema GOVERNANCE at multi-team scale is described as "trust infrastructure." The point is:
options: Cross-team trust is built on contracts, not personal relationships — governance encodes the contracts formally
Trust is built through Microsoft certification; without it, no team trusts another team's data
Trust is impossible at scale; governance accepts that and adds defensive walls
Trust requires GDPR consent forms; governance is the legal compliance layer
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-team trust is built on contracts, not personal relationships — governance encodes the contracts formally
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: at NordGrid scale (3 teams), trust was personal ("I know that engineer"). At GridUnion (12 teams), nobody knows everyone. Schema governance encodes contracts so trust transfers across teams.

questions.question: GridUnion's schema registry uses SEMANTIC VERSIONING (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH). MINOR increment means:
options: Backward-compatible additive change (new optional column) — consumers can migrate when convenient
Breaking change requiring consumer rewrites — major migration coordination required
Metadata-only update (description, owner) — no actual schema change at all
Capacity SKU change — the workspace was upgraded to a new tier
questions.correctAnswer: Backward-compatible additive change (new optional column) — consumers can migrate when convenient
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: SemVer for schemas. PATCH = metadata. MINOR = additive (backward compatible). MAJOR = breaking. Consumers know what to expect from the version number alone.

questions.question: A DATA CONTRACT extends a schema by including:
options: Freshness SLA, quality guarantees, owner, deprecation policy — what the consumer can DEPEND ON beyond just structure
Encryption keys — contracts manage cryptographic guarantees per consumer
Capacity allocation — contracts reserve compute resources for consumers
Source code — contracts include the producer's pipeline code for transparency
questions.correctAnswer: Freshness SLA, quality guarantees, owner, deprecation policy — what the consumer can DEPEND ON beyond just structure
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: schema = "what columns exist." Contract = "what guarantees the producer makes." Refresh time, quality bounds, deprecation timeline. Consumer commitments to plan against.

questions.question: Contract tests are run by the PRODUCER, not the consumer. Why?
options: The producer commits to the guarantees — they verify their own commitments before publishing data
Consumers run tests by default — producers only verify when consumers are unsatisfied
Microsoft mandates producer-side testing for all Fabric workloads regardless of pattern
Producer-side tests are 10× faster — consumer testing infrastructure can't scale
questions.correctAnswer: The producer commits to the guarantees — they verify their own commitments before publishing data
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: ownership. Producer commits → producer verifies. Consumer-side testing makes producers unaccountable for their own contracts. Producer side = clear responsibility.

questions.question: GridUnion's 90-DAY breaking change lifecycle is described as the COST OF breaking a contract. Why so long?
options: 12 consumers × hours of code update + testing + deployment per consumer × buffer for unexpected issues
Fabric requires 90-day lifecycle by platform policy for any breaking change
Microsoft pays consumers $1000/day for waiting; 90 days is the maximum compensation period
EU GDPR mandates 90 days for any consumer-affecting data structure change
questions.correctAnswer: 12 consumers × hours of code update + testing + deployment per consumer × buffer for unexpected issues
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: math, not bureaucracy. Each consumer needs time to: read the announcement, understand impact, update code, test, deploy. 12 consumers × multiple stages = 90 days minimum.

questions.question: GridUnion uses a UNIFIED WIDE schema for the cross-country meter table. The trade-off is:
options: Cross-country queries become trivial (just SELECT *), but the schema GROWS as countries add country-specific columns
The schema is locked forever — country-specific columns are forbidden once unified
Each country pays for the entire wide schema regardless of usage — billing fairness suffers
Spark can't handle wide schemas above 10 columns — the unified approach has a hard limit
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-country queries become trivial (just SELECT *), but the schema GROWS as countries add country-specific columns
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: simplicity vs growth. Unified wide = easy joins, easy cross-country queries. But every country adds columns → 9 columns becomes 15 over time. Storage and complexity grow.

questions.question: A schema change at the Delta TABLE LEVEL ripples through how many layers?
options: Four — Delta table + SQL endpoint + Warehouse views + semantic model — each must be updated coordinately
One — Fabric handles propagation automatically across all layers
Two — only Delta table and SQL endpoint; semantic models update on next refresh
Six — including Power BI workspaces, capacity assignments, and audit logs
questions.correctAnswer: Four — Delta table + SQL endpoint + Warehouse views + semantic model — each must be updated coordinately
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: schema changes are CROSS-LAYER events. Updating only the Delta table leaves SQL views, Warehouse views, and semantic models inconsistent. Coordinated multi-item deployment required.

questions.question: GridUnion's automated DRIFT DETECTION runs hourly. The point is:
options: Catch UNPLANNED schema changes — registry says column X, table has column Y; surface fast
Optimize storage — drift detection compresses tables that have grown out of registry bounds
Bill consumers — drift events trigger per-consumer charges based on usage patterns
Replace manual reviews — drift detection eliminates the need for the schema registry
questions.correctAnswer: Catch UNPLANNED schema changes — registry says column X, table has column Y; surface fast
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: drift = registry vs reality mismatch. Hourly check ensures unauthorized changes are caught in hours, not weeks. Compares actual schema to registered schema continuously.

questions.question: GridUnion's SELF-SERVICE schema registration for ADDITIVE changes was implemented because:
options: Manual entry by the platform team bottlenecked at 200+ tables — automation removes the human bottleneck
Self-service is required by GDPR — schema registration must happen without intermediation
Microsoft mandates self-service for any platform with more than 5 teams
It saves money — manual registration costs $100 per item; self-service is free
questions.correctAnswer: Manual entry by the platform team bottlenecked at 200+ tables — automation removes the human bottleneck
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: scale forces automation. Manual works for 10 tables. 200+ tables across 12 teams = platform team becomes bottleneck. Automation lets engineers register routine additive changes themselves.

questions.question: Schema GOVERNANCE is described as the "contract layer that makes data products trustworthy" — building on:
options: Module 1's data products with manifests/certification — Module 5 adds the schema-specific infrastructure
Module 7's CI/CD — schema governance is downstream of deployment automation
Module 9's monitoring — schema events are detected by the same monitoring system
Module 10's capacity planning — schema decisions are budgeted as capacity items
questions.correctAnswer: Module 1's data products with manifests/certification — Module 5 adds the schema-specific infrastructure
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: layered. Module 1 declares "this is a product." Module 5 enforces "and here's exactly what it guarantees about its schema." Both work together.

questions.question: A physical plan and the Spark UI show DIFFERENT things. The plan shows ___ ; the Spark UI shows ___:
options: Plan = INTENT (what Spark plans to do); Spark UI = REALITY (what actually happened with metrics)
Plan = old version; Spark UI = current version — they're different snapshots of the same thing
Plan = executor view; Spark UI = driver view — they're from different perspectives
Plan = compile time only; Spark UI = post-compile only — they happen in sequence
questions.correctAnswer: Plan = INTENT (what Spark plans to do); Spark UI = REALITY (what actually happened with metrics)
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: plan = blueprint. Spark UI = construction record. The plan says "broadcast hash join". The UI says "broadcast was 450MB". Discrepancies between intent and reality are where most performance bugs live.

questions.question: The DAGScheduler creates STAGE BOUNDARIES at:
options: Every shuffle exchange — narrow operations are pipelined; wide operations require new stages
Every action call — actions become stage boundaries automatically
Every 1000 transformations — Spark caps stages at this size for memory reasons
A configurable interval — `spark.dagScheduler.maxStageOps` controls stage size
questions.correctAnswer: Every shuffle exchange — narrow operations are pipelined; wide operations require new stages
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: stage = "do this without moving data between executors." Where data must move (shuffle), boundary occurs. Pipeline operators within a stage; new stage at every shuffle.

questions.question: Default `spark.locality.wait = 3s` was designed for HDFS but causes problems on cloud storage. Why?
options: On cloud storage, NODE_LOCAL doesn't exist (storage is remote) — waiting 3s before scheduling is pure idle time
Cloud storage caches data for 3 hours; the locality wait expires the cache prematurely
Spark's task scheduler runs out of memory if locality waits accumulate
Cloud regions have higher network jitter; 3s isn't enough wait for the locality optimizer
questions.correctAnswer: On cloud storage, NODE_LOCAL doesn't exist (storage is remote) — waiting 3s before scheduling is pure idle time
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: HDFS local disk = much faster than network. Wait makes sense. Cloud storage IS network — there's no NODE_LOCAL to wait for. The 3s default is pure waste.

questions.question: Push-based shuffle (Spark 3.2+) reverses the model by:
options: Map side PUSHES data to reduce-side executors as soon as it's ready, instead of reduce side pulling later
Reduce side pre-allocates partitions; push-based eliminates the reduce-side execution step
Driver pushes coordination metadata to all executors at once instead of pull-on-demand
Cluster manager pushes resource updates continuously instead of applications requesting
questions.correctAnswer: Map side PUSHES data to reduce-side executors as soon as it's ready, instead of reduce side pulling later
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: traditional pull = reduce side fetches. Push = map side ships immediately. Pre-aggregates, fewer network connections, faster failover. Default is still pull; push is for scale.

questions.question: GridUnion increased executor memory from 4 GB to 6 GB; query speed didn't change. Why?
options: The bottleneck was in EXECUTION memory (sort/join); the storage region grew but execution did not
Spark caps each executor at 4 GB regardless of configuration values supplied by the operator
JVM doesn't use additional memory beyond 4 GB without an explicit `-Xmx` heap adjustment
The query used Tungsten off-heap; on-heap allocation increases do not affect off-heap usage
questions.correctAnswer: The bottleneck was in EXECUTION memory (sort/join); the storage region grew but execution did not
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: unified memory model. 60/40 split. More total memory = more storage + more execution. If bottleneck is execution, only that share matters. Tuning fraction sometimes more important than total.

questions.question: "Spill (Disk)" in the Spark UI Stages tab means:
options: Tasks ran out of execution memory and serialized working data to local disk during sort/join
Cached DataFrames were evicted to disk because storage region was full
Output files were written to disk as the final result of the stage
The cluster's local SSDs were used for shuffle exchange (the standard happy path)
questions.correctAnswer: Tasks ran out of execution memory and serialized working data to local disk during sort/join
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: spill = "couldn't fit in memory, used disk instead." Not failure but a signal: per-task data is too large. Reduce data per partition or increase memory.

questions.question: A `*(2)` annotation on a physical plan operator means:
options: WHOLE-STAGE CODEGEN — fused with other operators into a single Java method, eliminating per-row dispatch
Estimated cost of 2 units in CBO's cost model relative to alternatives
Second to execute in the stage, scheduled after the first whole-stage barrier
Two executors process the operator in parallel for fault tolerance
questions.correctAnswer: WHOLE-STAGE CODEGEN — fused with other operators into a single Java method, eliminating per-row dispatch
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: same number = same fused method. Tungsten's answer to abstraction penalty: instead of operator-by-operator dispatch (slow), generate one tight loop fused across operators.

questions.question: Speculative execution duplicates a slow task on another executor. When does it MAKE THINGS WORSE?
options: When the slow task is slow due to LARGE INPUT skew; duplication wastes resources without finishing faster
When the cluster is mostly idle and has spare capacity; speculation has no effect in this state
When the task reads from local disk attached to that node; duplicate task loses data locality
When AQE is enabled in the configuration; speculative execution is automatically disabled then
questions.correctAnswer: When the slow task is slow due to LARGE INPUT skew; duplication wastes resources without finishing faster
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: speculation helps with hardware slowness (failing disk, GC). Hurts with data slowness (skew). Duplicating skewed task = same task, doubled cost, no faster.

questions.question: Default `spark.task.maxFailures = 4`. When does this cause production problems?
options: On deterministic failures (UDF bug) — 4 retries waste resources before the failure surfaces
On transient failures — 4 retries isn't enough; needs more aggressive retry counts
On any failure — 4 retries triggers Spark's circuit-breaker which kills the entire app
On task OOM — retries succeed in different memory pools; 4 is too few
questions.correctAnswer: On deterministic failures (UDF bug) — 4 retries waste resources before the failure surfaces
questions.explanation: Lesson 8 closing: retry policy is for TRANSIENT failures. Deterministic UDF bug fails 4 times deterministically. Wasted compute, delayed visibility. For pure-Spark code 4 is fine; with UDFs you may want fewer.

questions.question: GridUnion's execution-engine playbook is "the layer between optimizer and storage." Why does this matter?
options: Every performance problem ultimately manifests in execution — diagnosing through this layer reveals root causes
The execution engine is the only Spark layer with stable APIs; optimizer/storage change between versions
Cluster manager controls execution; the playbook documents those controls
Execution handles UDFs only; the playbook is for Python-Spark integration specifically
questions.correctAnswer: Every performance problem ultimately manifests in execution — diagnosing through this layer reveals root causes
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: optimizer chooses; storage stores; execution does the work. Slowness, OOM, fault tolerance — all are execution-engine phenomena. The playbook is the senior engineer's diagnostic reference.

questions.question: OneLake is Fabric's storage; underneath it is:
options: Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 — OneLake is a SaaS layer on top of ADLS Gen2
A custom Microsoft binary format unrelated to ADLS
Local SSD storage on each capacity node; OneLake is dedicated hardware
A Cosmos DB-based document store optimized for analytics queries
questions.correctAnswer: Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 — OneLake is a SaaS layer on top of ADLS Gen2
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: OneLake = ADLS Gen2 + Fabric integration. Same underlying technology, but tenant-scoped, governed, and integrated with Fabric workloads. Open formats stay portable.

questions.question: A Parquet "ROW GROUP" is:
options: A horizontal slice of the file — contiguous rows with all columns present, with its own footer stats
A vertical slice — a single column's data across all rows, stored together
A logical group of files within a directory — must be read together for consistency
A unit of replication — duplicated across cloud storage zones for durability
questions.correctAnswer: A horizontal slice of the file — contiguous rows with all columns present, with its own footer stats
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: row group = horizontal partition WITHIN a file. Each carries its own min/max stats per column, enabling row-group-level skipping during reads.

questions.question: A `region` column with 30 distinct values dictionary-encodes to ~48× compression. Why does ENCODING matter so much?
options: Encoding exploits column-level patterns BEFORE compression; encoded data compresses much better
Encoding is a substitute for compression — encoded tables don't need any codec
Encoding affects only column reads; compression affects file size
Encoding is mandatory for files >1 GB; codec is optional below
questions.correctAnswer: Encoding exploits column-level patterns BEFORE compression; encoded data compresses much better
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: schema design determines encoding effectiveness. 30-value column dictionary-encodes brilliantly. Same data as ms-precision timestamp = much worse compression.

questions.question: V-ORDER is a Fabric-specific Parquet write optimization. Trade-off:
options: Writes 5-10% slower; reads 30-50% faster — read-heavy workloads benefit dramatically
Writes faster; reads slower — V-Order optimizes ingestion at the cost of analytics
Storage doubles; performance the same — V-Order is purely a backup mechanism
No trade-off — V-Order is strictly better for all workloads
questions.correctAnswer: Writes 5-10% slower; reads 30-50% faster — read-heavy workloads benefit dramatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: write once, read many. Spending a bit more on write to get 30-50% read speedup is excellent ROI for analytical tables. Standard for production Lakehouses in Fabric.

questions.question: Delta's transaction log creates "CHECKPOINTS" every 10 versions. The checkpoint allows:
options: Fast snapshot read — instead of replaying every log entry from version 0, Spark reads checkpoint + recent entries
Backup mechanism — checkpoints are independently restorable if main log corrupts
Storage tier promotion — checkpoint files move to hot storage; older to cold automatically
Version pruning — checkpoints automatically delete data files older than checkpoint version
questions.correctAnswer: Fast snapshot read — instead of replaying every log entry from version 0, Spark reads checkpoint + recent entries
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: replay 1000 log entries = slow. Checkpoint summarizes "after version 100, here's the file list." Subsequent reads only replay 100→ to current. Linear vs constant time.

questions.question: GridUnion picked DELTA over Iceberg/Hudi because:
options: Fabric's Delta optimizations (Photon, predictive I/O) work with Delta only — vendor alignment
Delta is the only format with ACID transactions; Iceberg and Hudi lack this fundamental capability
Delta has lower licensing costs; Iceberg and Hudi require expensive commercial licensing fees
Delta supports SQL natively; Iceberg and Hudi are Python-only and have no SQL support
questions.correctAnswer: Fabric's Delta optimizations (Photon, predictive I/O) work with Delta only — vendor alignment
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: format choice often follows platform choice. Fabric + Delta + Photon = integrated stack. Other platforms with different vendors might pick differently.

questions.question: Cloud storage performance depends on "API interaction PATTERN, not raw bandwidth." Why?
options: A single sequential read hits modest throughput; speed comes from PARALLELIZING reads, not bigger pipes
Cloud bandwidth is uncapped on the network side; only requests-per-second affects observed throughput
Cloud APIs are async-only by design; sequential reads queue up internally at the storage service layer
Cloud uses HTTP/3 multiplexing under the hood; single connections automatically use available bandwidth
questions.correctAnswer: A single sequential read hits modest throughput; speed comes from PARALLELIZING reads, not bigger pipes
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: ADLS single-stream is 100-200 MB/s. With 100 parallel streams it's 10-20 GB/s. Pattern (parallelism, prefetch, batch sizing) determines achieved throughput.

questions.question: GridUnion's STORAGE diagnostic for slow scans starts with:
options: Verify partition pruning AND column pruning are both active in the plan — one is usually broken
Increase the capacity SKU — most scan slowness at scale is ultimately a capacity provisioning issue
Check the Delta log version count — high version counts slow reads when no checkpoint is active
Restart the entire workspace — Spark caches sometimes corrupt silently and need a manual refresh
questions.correctAnswer: Verify partition pruning AND column pruning are both active in the plan — one is usually broken
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: storage diagnostic = pushdown verification first. If pruning isn't working, the scan reads way more than necessary. Fix that before tuning lower-level parameters.

questions.question: CROSS-REGION OneLake reads (e.g. French workspace reading German Lakehouse) cost:
options: Higher latency AND egress charges — cross-region traffic is metered separately from intra-region
No additional cost — Microsoft includes cross-region in the standard capacity SKU
Capacity SKU upgrade — cross-region requires F128 or higher, lower SKUs are blocked
Compliance review — cross-region requires GDPR sign-off before each query
questions.correctAnswer: Higher latency AND egress charges — cross-region traffic is metered separately from intra-region
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: regions matter. EU North → EU West = network latency + egress fees per byte. Architecture decision: when do you replicate vs query cross-region? Cost analysis required.

questions.question: Storage architecture is "the FOUNDATION layer" because:
options: Every query, every pipeline, every consumer interacts with storage on every operation — choices propagate everywhere
It's the layer that costs the most money; financial governance focuses on storage exclusively
It has the strictest GDPR/SOX constraints; other layers are derivative
It changes most often; the rest of the platform depends on storage being current
questions.correctAnswer: Every query, every pipeline, every consumer interacts with storage on every operation — choices propagate everywhere
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: storage is unavoidable. Format, encoding, partitioning, compression — these affect EVERY query forever. Other layers can change pipeline-by-pipeline; storage is platform-wide.

questions.question: GridUnion's multi-tenancy uses workspace ISOLATION + capacity SEPARATION. The combination:
options: Workspace isolation handles data access; capacity separation handles compute fairness — different threats
They are equivalent — both achieve the same security goal through different mechanisms
Workspace isolation is required by Fabric; capacity separation is optional
Capacity separation replaces workspace isolation in advanced configurations
questions.correctAnswer: Workspace isolation handles data access; capacity separation handles compute fairness — different threats
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: defense in depth. Workspace = "who can read this data." Capacity = "who gets compute resources." Both needed: data isolation + compute fairness.

questions.question: EU regulations require COUNTRY-LEVEL data isolation. GridUnion enforces this through:
options: Country-coded workspaces (`ws_de_*`, `ws_fr_*`) + role assignments restricted to country teams
Geographic Azure regions only — country code in workspace name is informational
Microsoft Purview policies that auto-block cross-country queries
Single-country tables only — cross-country tables are forbidden by design
questions.correctAnswer: Country-coded workspaces (`ws_de_*`, `ws_fr_*`) + role assignments restricted to country teams
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: namespace + roles = country isolation. German team has roles only on `ws_de_*`. Country boundary is enforced through the workspace + role combination.

questions.question: Microsoft Purview at GridUnion scale automates:
options: Sensitivity classification + policy enforcement across 200+ items — manual labeling cannot scale
Cross-team approvals for every data access request submitted by an analyst or engineer in the org
Backup operations across all workspaces simultaneously, coordinated by a central governance team
Pipeline orchestration replacing Data Factory entirely for governance and compliance pipelines
questions.correctAnswer: Sensitivity classification + policy enforcement across 200+ items — manual labeling cannot scale
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Purview = governance automation. Auto-classify based on column patterns. Apply policies based on labels. At scale, manual classification of 200 items × 12 teams is impossible.

questions.question: In Unity Catalog, the most DANGEROUS privilege isn't ALL PRIVILEGES — it's:
options: GRANT OPTION — letting a team grant access to others enables unauthorized privilege propagation
CREATE CATALOG — only platform team should create catalogs
DROP TABLE — dropping deletes all data; more destructive than reading
SHOW PRIVILEGES — reading the privilege graph reveals access control structure
questions.correctAnswer: GRANT OPTION — letting a team grant access to others enables unauthorized privilege propagation
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: ALL PRIVILEGES = team can read/write their data. GRANT OPTION = they can give that to others, who can give it to others. Uncontrolled propagation. The most dangerous.

questions.question: GridUnion's "security policy as code" is implemented as:
options: JSON/YAML in Git defining policies; deployment scripts apply them to workspaces — reviewable
Manually-clicked workspace settings — code is the documentation only, no enforcement layer exists
Encrypted Word documents — policies are confidential and cannot be checked into a plain-text repo
Spark notebook code that engineers run weekly — policies are imperative scripts, not declarative
questions.correctAnswer: JSON/YAML in Git defining policies; deployment scripts apply them to workspaces — reviewable
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: policies in Git. Same review process as code. PRs propose policy changes; reviewers verify; merge applies. No more "we changed something but didn't document it."

questions.question: GDPR compliance audit at GridUnion is supported by:
options: AUDIT LOGS recording who accessed which item, when, with results — queryable proof of compliance
Annual surveys — engineers self-certify their data handling each year
Microsoft attestation — Microsoft confirms the platform meets GDPR for all customers
Encrypted backups — GDPR requires only that data is encrypted, not access tracked
questions.correctAnswer: AUDIT LOGS recording who accessed which item, when, with results — queryable proof of compliance
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: audit logs = proof. Compliance team queries logs to demonstrate access controls work. Without logs, "trust us" doesn't satisfy regulators.

questions.question: A SECURITY incident (potential PII exposure) requires what FIRST step?
options: CONTAIN — restrict access to the affected data immediately, BEFORE investigating root cause
INVESTIGATE — understand what happened completely before any other action
NOTIFY — inform regulators and affected users immediately, regardless of contained state
PATCH — fix the underlying vulnerability before doing anything else
questions.correctAnswer: CONTAIN — restrict access to the affected data immediately, BEFORE investigating root cause
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: stop the bleeding first. Investigation can come after. Notification has its own legal timeline. But containment must be immediate to limit blast radius.

questions.question: GridUnion's shared external dataset (consumer feedback) is shared across countries via:
options: A SINGLE shared workspace with read access for all 12 teams; one source of truth, no per-country copies
Per-country copies — each team has their own read of the shared data for performance
Direct access to source — every team queries the source system independently
A nightly export to each country's workspace — consistent point-in-time copies
questions.correctAnswer: A SINGLE shared workspace with read access for all 12 teams; one source of truth, no per-country copies
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: avoid copies. One workspace, 12 teams reading. Updates appear everywhere instantly. Per-country copies = drift, sync issues, multiplied storage cost.

questions.question: Security MONITORING at GridUnion uses anomaly detection on audit logs because:
options: At 200+ items × 80 engineers, manual log review is impractical — anomaly detection surfaces unusual patterns
Log review is forbidden for compliance reasons — only automated detection is allowed
Anomaly detection is required by Microsoft for any platform with > 5 teams
Manual review is too slow to catch active attacks; anomaly detection acts in real-time
questions.correctAnswer: At 200+ items × 80 engineers, manual log review is impractical — anomaly detection surfaces unusual patterns
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: scale forces automation. Manual review of audit logs works for 5 engineers. 80 engineers × 200 items = millions of events. Anomaly detection finds the unusual without humans reading every log.

questions.question: Security architecture is described as "NEVER COMPLETE" because:
options: New threats and regulations emerge continuously; the architecture evolves as the threat model evolves
Spark releases security patches monthly; the architecture must be updated with each release
Cloud providers change default security settings; architecture is rewritten with each change
Compliance frameworks expire after 12 months; security architecture is annually re-certified with rewrites
questions.correctAnswer: New threats and regulations emerge continuously; the architecture evolves as the threat model evolves
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: security is a moving target. New attack classes emerge. New regulators set new requirements. The architecture must support evolution — fixed designs eventually expire.

questions.question: GridUnion's evolution from Lambda to Hybrid streaming was motivated by:
options: Hybrid eliminates batch-streaming code duplication via shared transforms; Lambda forces two codebases
Hybrid is mandated by GDPR; Lambda violates EU streaming data requirements
Hybrid is 70% cheaper than Lambda; cost reduction drove the migration
Microsoft deprecated Lambda support in Fabric 2024; Hybrid was the only option
questions.correctAnswer: Hybrid eliminates batch-streaming code duplication via shared transforms; Lambda forces two codebases
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: Lambda = two pipelines, two codebases that drift. Hybrid = same transforms, two entry points. Best of both worlds.

questions.question: GridUnion's Eventstream topology has BRANCHING and JOINING. The capability lets:
options: A single source feed multiple processing paths; multiple sources combine into a unified stream
A linear stream from source to sink; branching/joining is forbidden in Fabric Eventstreams
Streaming queries to share Spark sessions; topology is purely about resource sharing
The Spark version to be customized per branch; topology controls Spark deployments
questions.correctAnswer: A single source feed multiple processing paths; multiple sources combine into a unified stream
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: complex topologies enable real architecture. SCADA telemetry → branched into anomaly detection + KQL ingestion + Lakehouse archival. Same source, three destinations.

questions.question: MULTI-STREAM CORRELATION (joining 2 streams) requires:
options: A WATERMARK on each stream to bound state — without watermarks, state grows unbounded
Both streams to use the same Eventstream — cross-stream is forbidden
A pre-defined join schema — dynamic correlation isn't supported
Spark Premium licensing — multi-stream correlation isn't in standard Fabric
questions.correctAnswer: A WATERMARK on each stream to bound state — without watermarks, state grows unbounded
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: stream-stream joins need watermarks. Without them, every event waits forever for a potential match. State explodes. Watermark = "after this delay, give up matching."

questions.question: Exactly-once delivery is described as "a system property, not a feature." Why?
options: Requires source, processing engine, AND sink to cooperate via shared checkpoint coordination
Spark configures it automatically; calling it a feature is technically incorrect
It's a guarantee provided by Kafka alone; engine and sink aren't involved
It's a configuration toggle in Spark 3.5+; flipping it on enables platform-wide
questions.correctAnswer: Requires source, processing engine, AND sink to cooperate via shared checkpoint coordination
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: three links in the chain. Kafka must replay correctly. Spark must checkpoint correctly. Delta must use idempotent writes. Break any link and exactly-once degrades to at-least-once.

questions.question: KQL at enterprise scale (43M events/day) requires careful schema design. The KEY decision:
options: Choose ingestion-time partitioning aligned with query patterns — KQL queries hit the partition first
Use a single huge table — KQL handles arbitrary scale without schema considerations
Encrypt every column — KQL's default is unencrypted, which fails compliance
Replicate across all 4 countries — single-region KQL doesn't scale to 43M events
questions.correctAnswer: Choose ingestion-time partitioning aligned with query patterns — KQL queries hit the partition first
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: scale-driven schema. KQL's strength is time-series. Partition by time bucket aligned with query patterns. Wrong partitioning = full-table scans even for time-bounded queries.

questions.question: STREAMING schema evolution is HARDER than batch because:
options: No deployment window — pipeline runs continuously; schema changes mid-run vs scheduled batch deployments
Streaming caches schema in driver memory; restart is forbidden during peak hours
Catalyst recompiles streaming plan from scratch; batch reuses existing plan
Streaming pipelines reject any schema change at runtime; the only option is delete + recreate
questions.correctAnswer: No deployment window — pipeline runs continuously; schema changes mid-run vs scheduled batch deployments
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: batch has natural windows between runs. Streaming runs forever — schema changes happen WHILE processing. Coordinated stop/migrate/restart is harder than scheduled batch redeployment.

questions.question: STREAMING STATE growing unbounded is solved by:
options: WATERMARKS — events older than the watermark are dropped, bounding state to recent data
Manual cleanup every hour — batch jobs delete old state files
Larger executor memory — state grows linearly so memory must scale
Disabling streaming joins — state-heavy operations can't run in streaming mode
questions.correctAnswer: WATERMARKS — events older than the watermark are dropped, bounding state to recent data
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: bounded state via watermarks. State store keeps recent data; older data is finalized and discarded. Without watermarks, state grows forever.

questions.question: STREAMING DR (disaster recovery) requires:
options: Cross-region checkpoint replication + Eventstream multi-region failover — streaming's DR pattern
Daily snapshots only — streaming pipelines follow the same DR pattern as nightly batch jobs do
Manual failover only — streaming pipelines cannot auto-failover safely without data loss risk
Microsoft handles streaming DR automatically — customers do not configure anything themselves
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-region checkpoint replication + Eventstream multi-region failover — streaming's DR pattern
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: streaming DR is harder than batch DR. Continuous state must replicate continuously. Eventstreams must failover without losing offsets. Specific patterns required.

questions.question: STREAMING COSTS are driven by:
options: Constant CU consumption — streaming runs 24/7 vs batch's scheduled bursts; per-day cost dominates
Per-event charges — Microsoft bills $0.001 per Eventstream message
Storage replication — streaming requires triple-region replication by policy
Network egress — streaming events leave Azure for processing on customer servers
questions.correctAnswer: Constant CU consumption — streaming runs 24/7 vs batch's scheduled bursts; per-day cost dominates
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 24h × 365 days vs 30 minutes × 365 days. Streaming consumes capacity continuously. Cost optimization = reduce per-event compute, not just total volume.

questions.question: A streaming platform is "as reliable as its WEAKEST OPERATIONAL practice." Why?
options: Sound mechanisms (exactly-once, state stores) need rigorous practices (testing, monitoring); a weak practice undoes everything
Streaming reliability is determined entirely by the cluster manager; operational practices have no effect
Spark's streaming engine self-corrects all errors; operational practices are convention but not requirement
